MISCELLANEOUS  RECIPES 


MILK  (Le  Lait)  r  ' 

*  ■  •.  .  •  ■: ■''■)& 

Milk  is  highly  nutritious  and  is  adapted  to  promote 
the  growth  of  children  and  youth,  and  the  fact  that 
nature  has  ordained  it  as  the  natural  food  for  infants, 
is  no  mean  guaranty  of  its  ability.  Milk  promotes 
sleep  and  therefore  is  better  for  supper,  especially  for 
children  and  probably  for  the  wakeful.  Sour  milk  and 
sweetened  buttermilk  are  nutritious  and  healthy— more 
so  than  sweet  milk,  because  milk  must  be  curdled  be¬ 
fore  it  can  be  digested. 

Some  consumptives  attribute  their  recovery  to  the 
use  of  buttermilk. '  Sweet  cider  added  to  curdled  milk 
makes  a  delicious  and  wholesome  drink.  Scientists 
have  been  debating  a  long  time  to  find  out  in  which 
manner  milk  should  be  taken,  boiled  or  fresh  from 
the  cow.  Some  people  complain,  after  drinking  milk 
that  they  are  deranged,  but  that  comes  from  drinking 
too  hurriedly.  .  It  is  necessary  to  consume  a  cup 
of  milk  in  five  minutes  time.  Milk  is  used  in  the. 
culinary  department  as  a  basis  for  cream  soups, 
vegetables,  sauces,  pastry  and  ice  creams.  Different 
milk  employed  in  alimentation  are:  cow,  goat, 

mares,  ewe  and  reindeer.  Sterilized  milk  is  milk  re¬ 
duced  to  the  consistency  of  cream  by  evaporating  in 
vacuum  and  then  thoroughly  sterilized. 

To  keep  fresh  milk  from  decomposing  put  65  grains 
bicarbonate  of  soda  to  one  quart  of  milk.  After  certain 
preparation  of  cream  and  milk,  butter  and  cheese  are 
made  in  every  part  of  the  world.  The  Russian  makes 
Kumyss  and  brandy  with  mares  milk.  The  Turks  make 
their  caimak  or  cream  with  milk  of  ewes. 

OIL  (Huile) 

Oil  replaces  butter  in  certain  countries.  It  is  ex¬ 
tracted  from  fruits,  grain  seeds  and  plants.  The  best 
oil  comes  from  olives,  and  the  best  producing  countries 
are  Nice,  France,  California  and  Lucca,  Italy. 

The  second  quality  of  oil  is  made  from  walnuts,  hazel- 


—  449  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


nuts,  almonds,  sesame,  peanuts,  flaxseed,  cotton,  poppy 
seeds  and  colza. 

Always  use  the  first  quality  oil  in  everything  edible. 

To  prevent  oil  from  becoming  rancid,  put  in  acid 
suflophenique  2%. 

PERPETUAL  VINEGAR  (Vinaigre  Perpetual) 

Buy  a  small  keg  of  “mother”  vinegar.  Draw  a  few 
quarts  and  make  aromatic  vinegar.  Replace  quantity 
taken  out  by  wine  or  cider.  Close  keg  with  a  piece  of 
linen.  Keep  in  cellar  40  to  50  degrees  Fahr. 

How  to  make  divers  aromatic  vinegar. 

Tarragon  Leaves  1  lb.,  6  qt.  vinegar,  15  days. 

Celery,  ceilery  seeds  1  lb,,  5  qt.  vinegar,  8  days, — 
etraiped.  i 

Hunter  Style,  Elderflowers  1  ounce,  fresh  juniper 
seeds  10  ounces,  savory  1  y2  ounces,  vinegar  3  qt.,  let 
stand  15  days,  strain  and  filter. 

Aromatics,  Slice  5  heads  of  garlic  and  20  shallots,  bay 
leaves  10,  Cloves  20,  Salad  burnett  2  ounces,  mint  1 
ounce,  balm  %  ounce,  crushed  pepper  %  ounce,  coarse 
salt  7  ounces,  vinegar  10  quarts,  let  stand  40  days,  stir 
occasionally,  filter,  put  in  bqttles. 

PRESERVES  (Conserves) 

Different  methods  of  conserving  are:  The  dessication 
of  fruits,  vegetables  and  meats,  the  smoked,  dried  and 
cooked  fruits  put  in  alcohol,  the  juice  of  meats  and 
fruits,  such  as  loaves  and  tablets;  the  jams,  marmalades 
and  jellies;  pickled  meat,  fish,  vegetables  and  butter. 
Jellies,  grease  and  oils  are  conserved  in  vinegar,  sugar 
and  alcohol,  and  must  be  tightly  closed  so  no  air  can 
penetrate  The  best  method  of  conserving  is  the 
Appert  system,  which  is:  boiling  of  the  cans  or  steam¬ 
ing  of  the  jars.  The  method  of  to-day  is  the  refri- 


—  450 


PRESERVES 


gerator*  All  articles  put  in  the  refrigerator  should  be 
absolutely  fresh.  Experts  should  he  named  by  the 
Board  of  Health,  to  inspect  all  food,  to  see  that  it  is 
fresh  before  putting  in  cold  storage. 

Appert  System 

Oook  or  scald  the  meat,  vegetable  or  fruit,  put  in  a 
bottle  well  corked  and  tie  with  a  string.  Put  in  a  large 
pot  with  hot  or  cold  water.  Let  boil  from  10  to  30 
minutes  according  to  the  substance.  Put  straw  around 
the  bottles  in  boiling,  so  they  will  not  crack. 

Preserved  eggs  (CEufs  conserves) 

The  perfect  conservation  of  eggs  is  not  yet  known. 
The  method  most  employed  is:  Cooked  in  lime  paraffin, 
silicate  of  soda,  or  refrigerator. 

Preserved  by  Coction,  Dip  very  fresh  eggs  2  minutes 
in  boiling  water,  let  cool  immediately;  keep  in  sand 
in  a  cool  place. 

Preserved  in  lime  water,  Quick  lime  2  lbs.,  30  quarts 
water,  put  eggs  in  jar,  dissolve  lime  in  water,  so  that 
all  of  the  eggs  are  covered  over  with  the  solution. 
Preserved  in  Silicate  of  Soda,  Put  eggs  in  jar,  cover 
with  solution  of  silicate  of  Soda  and  water,  let  stand 
in  solution  10  minutes,  then  take  each  egg  separately 
and  put  between  two  sticks,  when  dry  turn  over  and 
brush  with  the  solution.  Keep  on  husks  of  grain. 
Preserved  in  Refrigerator,  latest  style,  Put  boxes  in 
cold  storage,  or  break  the  eggs  in  a  can  or  in  a  solu¬ 
tion  of  salicylate  of  soda,  or  the  yellow  of  eggs  dried. 

Preserved  Olives,  green  or  ripe. 

Put  6  per  cent  of  wood  ashes  and  equal  quantity  of 
slack  lime,  let  olives  stand  48  hours,  change  the  water 
©very  24  hours  for  10  days,  then  in  the  following  pickled 
composition,  which  is:  12  quarts  water,  1  pound  coarse 
salt,  fennel,  bay  leaves,  coriander;  'let  boil  1  hour,  strain. 
Put  olives  in  this  composition  when  it  is  cold.  The 
olives  can  be  eaten  after  15  days.  Olives  put  in  a  jar 
with  olive  oil  and  garlic  for  3  or  4  days  are  very- 
appetizing.  -  a  -;=  -  ,r!"° 


—  451  —

HOW  TO  MAKE  SOAP  (Savons  mou  et  dur) 

Save  all  of  the  grease  from  cooking,  clarify,  and  you 
have  your  daily  common  soap. 

Composition,  hard  soap:  1  qt.  water  for  13  ounces 
of  lye,  dissolve,  add  w.  6  lbs.  grease,  warm  and  melt, 
pour  in  a  suitable  mould.  Comp,  soft  soap:  2  galls, 
water  for  13  ounces  of  lye,  boil,  dissolve,  add  6  lbs. 
grease  and  1  gall,  water,  boil  slowly  until  the  soap  is 
transparent,  add  9  galls,  hot  water  and  stir  all  together. 

JAVEL  WATER 

Composition:  13  ounces  of  lye,  dissolve  w.  10  galls, 
water  and  2  lbs.  washing  soda. 

SYRUP  OF  FRUITS  (Sirops  de  Fruits) 

SYrup  flavored  w.  different  sorts  of  fruits. 

Use  any  kind  of  fruit,  and  proceed  in  this  manner: 
Increase  or  reduce  the  amount  of  sugar,  according  to 
the  juice  of  the  fruit  you  use.  Composition:  Choose 
very  ripe  fruit,  crush  and  let  repose  in  basin  for  24 
hours,  then  press  the  juice  out.  For  2  quarts  of  juice 
Y2  pound  sugar  or  more,  y2  stick  of  vanilla.  Macerate 
for  1  month  in  demijohn  or  small  barrel,  let  draw  slowly 
until  clear,  then  filter  through  paper. 

RASPBERRY  SYRUP  WITH  VINEGAR 

This  syrup  is  used  for  dysentery  and  is  anti-choleric. 
Crush  raspberries  with  vinegar,  according  to  quantity. 
For  2  qts.  of  juice  add  4  lbs.  of  sugar,  let  stand  15 
days,  then  bottle  and  close  hermetically. 

SYRUP  OF  ROGES  AND  VIOLETS 

Choose  roses  or  violets,  highly  perfumed.  Composi¬ 
tion:  2  lbs.  petals,  2 %  qts.  of  boiling  water.  Put  petals 
in  a  silver  or  enamel  vase,  pour  boiling  water  on,  close 
tight’,  let  macerate  18  hours,  strain  in  fine  cloth,  then 
press  quickly,  let  stand  4  hours,  always  tightly  closed. 
Take  out  of  vase,  and  add  for  every  2  lbs.  of  infusion 
3%  lbs.  of  white  sugar  candy,  then  sugar  and  the 
infusion  together  in  the  vase,  and  do  not  let  evaporate, 


—  454 


MISCELLANEOUS 


then  put  in  bain-marie,  and  let  warm  slowly  until  sugar 
is  dissolved,  take  out,  let  cooil,  always  keeping  tightly 
closed.  Put  in  dry,  clean  bottles  and  cork  well,  then 
place  in  a  closet,  where  there  is  no  light,  nor  any  noise. 
The  rose  syrup  can  be  sligthly  colored  with  carmine. 

COLD  SYRUP  (Sirop  a  froid) 

For  uncooked  sugar  syrup  use  2  lbs.  of  sugar  and 
1  qt.  water,  which  makes  34  degrees  syrup. 

Hot  same  quantity,  makes  32  degrees  syrup.  Same 
w.  114  lbs.  sugar  and  %  qt.  of  water  makes  22  degrees, 
syrup. 

SUGAR  AND  ICING,  how  to  make  (Sucre  et  Fondant) 

Cooked  sugar  39  degrees.  Work  on  marble  slab  w. 
acetic  acid.  Work  with  palm  of  the  hand,  add  coloring, 
flavoring  or  chocolate  the  last  moment. 

ROYAL  ICING 

Use  icing  sugar  and  white  eggs,  beat  well.  Duck 
eggs  are  preferred,  as  they  make  a  whiter  icing.  Cooked 
icing:  1  lb.  sugar  cooked  37  degrees,  add  4  whipped 
whites  of  eggs. 

TEA  (The) 

Green  tea  is  more  stimulating  than  black.  It  pro¬ 
duces  wakefulness  and  cramps  in  the  stomach.  It  is 
an  energetic  stimulant  and  should  be  used  moderately. 
In  cold  countries  like  England,  Holland,  America  and 
Russia,  tea  replaces  wine  by  its  powerful  stimulating 
qualities.  The  Chinese  prepare  tea  to  perfection  and  for 
them  it  is  a  national  secret. 

Black  Tea 

Pekoe  or  Pak-ho,  Pekoe  d’assam,  Pekoe  orange,  Con¬ 
go,  Koong-foo,  Pouchou  Paou-chung,  S’ouchon  Seaou- 
chung,  Tea  Bohea,  Wood-e  of  Fokien,  Bovi-bou  of  Can¬ 
ton,  Oolong,  English,  in  fusion  w.  boiling  water  5  to 
10  min.  according  to  strength  you  desire. 

Green  Tea 

Hyson  He-chun,  Poudre  A  canon,  Choo-cha,  Imperial 
Hyson  S'choulang,  Hysoon  Junior,  Yu-tseen,  Tonkay  tun- 
he,  Hyson  skin.

GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


WATER  (L’JEau) 

Iced  or  cold  water  taken  in  summer  aids  the  diges¬ 
tion,  but  it  is  not  prudent  to  drink  if  one  is  perspiring. 
Warm  water  is  effervescent  and  produces  vomiting. 
Hot  water,  with  a  pinch  of  salt,  a  little  lemon  juice  or 
sugar,  acts  upon  the  digestive  organs  and  kills  the 
germs.  But  no  matter  in  what  form  water  is  taken, 
it  must  be  potable.  Water  is  an  efficient  promoter  of 
normal  action,  of  universal  life.  Of  its  wonderful  heat¬ 
ing  virtues — of  which  it  contains  a  large  proportion — 
is  probably  the  chief  instrumentality. 

TO  PRESERVE  WATER  (Conservation  de  1’Eau) 

The  most  simple  and  cheap  way  to  conserve  potable 
water  is  to  put  in  a  piece  of  charcoal,  according  to 
quantity. 

TO  CLARIFY  MUDDY  WATER 
(Clarification  de  I’Eau  Trouble) 

If  you  have  no  filter,  use  y2  ounce  of  alum  to  100 
quarts  of  water;  the  water  will  become  clear  in  a  few 
seconds,  as  all  of  the  sediment  will  go  to  the  bottom. 

ARTIFICIAL  MINERAL  WATER  (Eaux  Artificielles) 

Dissolve  in  water,  salts  from  the  natural  springs  and 
sparkling  water. 

NATURAL  MINERAL  WATERS 
(Eaux  Minerales) 

Mineral  waters  are  divided  in  5  classes  such  as:  sul¬ 
phur,  chlorate,  bicarbonate,  sulphate  and  ferruginous. 
Mineral  waters  not  classed  are:  alkaline,  natural  salts 
from  the  springs  and  iodures.  They  are  other  spring 
waters  not  classified.  In  the  Duchy  of  Nassau,  there 
is  a  natural  Seltzer  water  spring. 

YEAST  FOR  PASTES  (Levure  pour  Pates) 

When  yeast  can  not  be  obtained,  make  it  yourself 
in  this  manner:  farinaceous  potatoes  2  pounds,  4  ounces 
molasses,  y2  pint  beer.  Boil  potatoes,  peel,  mash  w. 
warm  water,  add  molasses  when  fermentation  sets  in, 
then  beer,  stir.  Keep  in  warm  place,  ready  for  use  in 
24  hours.  This  yeast  is  good  also  for  bread.  (See  bread 
and  baking  powder.) 


—  456  — 


THE  C  HE  MI S  T  R  Y  O F  G  O  OKI N  G 


THE  CHEMISTRY  OF  COdKlNii 


MODERN  science  has,  of  late  years,  thrown .  much  light 
on  culinary  operations;',  explaining  the  unknown  causes 
of  many  welbknown  effects,  and  giving  a  reasbn  for  certain 
rules  in  dressing  our  food,  which  forbids  caprice  or  dissent 
from  them.  It  teaches  us  the  constituent  parts  of  the  sub' 
stance  we  use,  and  the  chemical  action  of  fire  on  them,  and 
also  estimates  the  amount  of  nutrition  to  be  derived  from 
each  separate  article  of  food. 

The  flesh  of  animals  is  chiefly  composed  of  fibres,  fat, 
albumen,  gelatine  and  osmazome.  The  fibres  of  meat  cannot 
be  dissolved ;  they  may  be  seen  as  the  remainder  of  the  beef 
stewed  down  for  strong  beef  tea,  when  every  substance 
contained  in  cells  covered  with  a  fine  membrane  which,  like 
the  fibres,  cannot  be  dissolved.  Heat  bursts  the  cells,  and 
fat  melts  from  them.  It  is  light  and  rises  to  the  top  of  the 
water.  Albumen  is  a  substance  exactly  similar  to  the  white 
of  an  egg.  In  raw  meat  it  is  liquid,  as  in  an  uncooked 
egg  and  it  surrounds  each  fibre.  Its  use  is  to  keep  the  fibres 
from  contracting  and  hardening  under  the  influence  of  heat, 
for  albumen  thickens  or  coagulates  when  heated  and  thus 
preserves  the  fibres  from  growing  hard,  and  keeps  the  meat 
(when  well  cooked)  tender.  Of  course,  therefore,  the  more 
albumen  the  flesh  contains  the  tenderer  it  is.  Young  animals 
have  more  albumen  in  the  fluids  which  the  tissues  of  the 
muscles  contain  than  old  ones  have,  and  consequently  their 
flesh  is  more  tender:  but  as  this  greater  quantity  of  albumen 
in  it  hardens  and  coagulates  when  exposed  to  heat,  their 
flesh  has  little  or  no  gravy,  and  is  called  white  meat.  Beef 
and  mutton  are  brown  and  juicy  because  they  have  less  ah 
bumen.  Albumen  can  be  dissolved  in  cold  or  tepid  water,  but 
it  will  coagulate  in  hot  or  boiling  water.  Therefore,  if  you  put 
your  meat  into  the  stockpot  when  the  water  is  boiling,  the 
albumen  will  harden  and  prevent  the  gelatine  and  osmazome 
from  dissolving  and  being  drawn  out  to  give  substance  and 
flavor  to  the  stock.  If  the  meat  that  is  to  be  boli*d  is  put 
into  hot  water  and  is  made  to  boil  up  quickly,  the  albumen 
will  harden  and  remain  in  the  meat,  with  the  same  effect 
of  retaining  the  gelatine  and  osmaz,ome,  Liebig  prefers  this 

—  457.  — 


CANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


mode  of  boiling.  Gelatine  is  the  glutinous  matter  in  meat 
which,  when  extracted  and  let  get  cold,  makes  the  jelly  you 
see  in  good  stock.  Bones  contain  more  gelatine  than  flesh 
does.  Two  ounces  of  bones  contain  as  much  gelatine  as 
one  pound  of  meat.  Gelatine  is  utterly  without  flavor,  but 
very  strengthening.  Powdered  bones  may  be  dissolved  com' 
pletely  into  gelatine.  Osmazome  is  that  part  of  the  substance 
of  meat  which  gives  it  flavor  and  perfume.  Flesh  of  old 
animals  has  more  osmazome  and  consequently  more  flavor. 
Our  readers  will  at  once  acknowledge  that  the  brown  meats 
have  more  flavor  than  the  white.  Under  the  influence  of 
dry  heat  (i.  e.,  roasting)  osmazome  acquires  higher  properties 
and  gives  forth  a  better  flavor. 

Meat  is  “under'dressed”  when  it  has  only  been  suffn 
ciently  heated  through  to  coagulate  the  albumen  it  contains. 
It  is  perfectly  done  when  it  has  been  heated  throughout  to  a 
temperature  sufficiently  strong  to  coagulate  the  coloring 
matter  of  the  blood.  It  is  “too  much  dressed”  when  the 
heat  has  continued  long  enough  to  harden  the  fibres.  The 
juices  of  flesh  should  be  kept  in  (if  the  meat  is  dressed  for 
eating,  not  for  soup),  as  they  contain  much  nutritive  matter, 
and  the  succulence  of  the  joint  depends  on  their  retention  in 
it.  If  meat  is  exposed  to  a  “quick”  heat  the  albumen  on 
the  surface  will  coagulate  and  “keep  in”  the  juices  and  the 
meat  will  be  lightly  dressed.  This  is  a  peculiarly  English 
mode  of  cooking,  for  which  foreigners  occasionally  style  us, 
jestingly,  “Cannibal.” 

It  is  essential  that  cooks  should  remember  that  there  is 
a  certain  amount  of  waste  in  meat  when  exposed  to  the 
action  of  fire,  i.  e.,  when  cooked.  The  fatter  the  ,meat  the 
greater  the  waste  in  roasting.  It  has  been  estimated  by  our 
very  clever  domestic  chemists  that  fresh  beef  and  mutton 
lose  as  follows:  Beef,  out  of  four  pounds,  will  lose  one 
pound  in  boiling,  one  pound  three  ounces  in  baking,  one 
pound  five  ounces  in  roasting.  Mutton  loses  out  of  four 
pounds,  fourteen  ounces  in  boiling,  one  pound  four  ounces 
in  baking,  one  pound  six  ounces  in  roasting.  Thus  the 
reader  will  perceive  that  a  joint  weighing  eight  pounds  will 
lose  in  roasting  two  pounds  ten  ounces,  but  the  dripping 
will  remain  over.  Boiling  is  evidently  a  cheaper  mode  of 
cookery  (especially  in  England),  as  less  substance  is  lost,  and 
the  quantity  abstracted,  having  passed  into  the  water,  renders 

—  458  — 


THE  CHEMISTRY  OF  COOKIN 


V/ 


that  available  for  soup  or  broth,  with  a  little  additional  stock, 

The  knowledge  of  these  effects  of  heat  on  animal  sub' 
stances  will  guide  the  cook  in  her  proceedings,  whether  the 
family  whom  she  serves  prefer  their  meat  well  dressed  or 
underdressed.  It  will  be  of  especial  use  in  that  most  chem- 
ical  of  all  operations,  making  soup,  or  for  making  the  stock 
for  it,  and  for  gravies. 

The  French  have  always  possessed  an  instinctive  know¬ 
ledge  of  the  chemistry  of  cooking,  hence  their  unrivalled 
excellence  in  the  art.  We  subjoin  as  extract  on  the  subject, 
translated  from  “L’Art  de  la  Cuisine  Frangaise,”  by  the 
celebrated  cook  of  the  Prince  Regent  Careme:  “The  good 
housewife,”  he  says,  “puts  her  meat  into  an  earthen  stock 
pot  and  pours  cold  water  on  it  in  the  proportion  of  two 
quarts  to  three  pounds  of  the  beef.  She  sets  it  at  the  side  of 
the  fire  and  unconsciously  performs  an  act  of  chemical  skill. 
The  pot  grows  gradually  hot  and  as  the  water  heats  it  dilates 
the  muscular  fibres  of  the  flesh  by  dissolving  easily,  and  rise 
to  the  surface  of  the  water  in  light  foam  or  scum,  while  the 
osmazome,  which  is  the  savoury  juice  of  the  meat,  dissolving 
little  by  little,  adds  flavour  to  the  broth.  By  this  simple 
proceeding  of  “slow”  cooking,  the  housewife  obtains  a 
savoury  and  nourishing  broth  and  a  “bouilli,”  (boiled  meat) 
tender  and  with  a  good  flavour.” 

These  are  the  results  of  the  chemical  action  of  fire; 
“but,”  he  continues,  “by  placing  the  “pot-au-feu”  on  too 
hot  a  fire,  it  boils  too  soon;  the  albumen  coagulates  and 
hardens;  the  water,  not  having  had  the  time  necessary  to 
penetrate  the  meat,  hinders  the  osmazome  from  disengaging 
itself,  and  the  sad  result  is  that  you  have  only  a  hard  piece 
of  boiled  meat  and  a  broth  without  flavour  or  goodness. 
A  little  fresh  water  poured  into  the  pot  at  intervals  helps 
the  scum  to  rise  more  abundantly.” 

This  practical  explanation  will,  we  trust,  show  you  the 
importance  of  knowing  something  of  the  chemistry  of  cook¬ 
ing.

G  ANGEL’ S  r  C  U  L  IN  ARY  ENCYCLOPE  D  I  A 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 

Tile  ■  destiny  oi  nations  defend  on  their  diet. 

— BRILil/AT  SAVARIN. 

TFHE  GASTRONOMIC  taste  changes  with  the  progress  of 
*a  people.  Without  exactly  assenting  either  to  the  gastro¬ 
nomic  Frenchman,  American,  English,  Italian,  German  or 
Russian,  we  are  of  the  opinion  that  the  civilization  of  a 
people  or  of  any  age  may  be  ascertained  by  the  style  of  its 
cuisine.  Man  has  been  defined  as  a  living  animal  and  pre¬ 
eminence  in  the  art  is  assuredly  a  sign  of  his  advancement. 

It  is  historically  known  that  civilization  and  culinary 
existed  with  the  Greeks  to  a  high  degree  of  perfection 
centuries  ago.  The  French  have  always  been  possessed  of 
an  instinctive  knowledge  of  the  chemistry  of  cooking,  hence 
then;  excellence  was  unrivalled. 

In  the  meantime  the  gastronomic  art  had  been  making 
great  progress.  It  took  a  long  step  forward  in  the  reign  of 
Louis  the  Fourth  who  was  a  gourmand  in  addition  to  his 
other  distinctive  characteristics. 

From  this  period  the  cuisine  entered  a  new  era  which 
dates  from  the  immigration  of  chefs  from  divers  countries 
of  Europe.  Such  disciples  as  “Antonin  Careme”  and  his 
successors  in  the  culinary  art.  They  have  produced  the 
refinement  and  delicacy  of  our  days  which  we  call  “Cuisine 
Modcrne.” 

PLAIN  DIRECTIONS  FOR  ROASTING 

How  to  Roast — Roasting  meat,  though  of  the  commonest 
modes  of  dressing  it,  is  by  no  means  an  easy  task.  Roast 
meat  is  too  often  sent  to  the  table  nearly  raw,  or  dried  up 
till  there  is  scarcely  any  gravy  in  it.  Now,  “good”  roasting 
consists  in  dressing  the  joint  thoroughly  and  yet  retaining 
its  juice  in  it.  The  French  motto — “L’on  devient  cuisinier 
mais  l’on  nait  rotisseur”— you  must  not  forget  that  you 
become  a  cook,  and  that  you  are  born  a  roastcook. 

The  cook  should  prepare  the  fire  some  little  time  before 
he  puts  the  meat  down.  The  grate  should  be  left  out 
sufficiently  wide  to  take  in  the  whole  size  of  the  joint,  with 
a  margin  to  spare  on  each  side,  and  the  fire  should  be  “so” 
good  so  as  not  to  require  making  up  during  the  time  the 
joint  is  roasting.  It  should  be  sufficiently  large  to  be  of  an 

—  460  7- 


C  U  LINARY  EVOLUTION 


equal  strength  all  the  time  the  meat  is  dressing,  aided  by 
a  large  coal  put  on  the  top  of  it  occasionally.  A  great  deal 
of  the  success  in  roasting  will  depend  on  the  heat  and  good' 
ness  of  the  fire. 

Note — The  best  way  of  roasting  any  meat  is  on  a  spit, 
but  that  mode  is  quite  forgotten  these  days.-  Every  house 
uses  the  coal,  gas  or  electric  oven. 

Place  the  meat  on  the  roast  pan  in  a  hot  oven  and  baste 
frequently  with  the  butter,  lard  of  grease,  according  to  your 
like.  After  it  is  cooked  and  has  taken  brown  color,  remove 
the  grease  and  moisten  with  stock  or  water. 

When  it  is  half  done  move  it  gradually  nearer  to  the 
fire  for  it  to  be  well  browned.  If  the  meat  were  to  be  put 
close  to  the  fire  at  first  it  would  dry  up  and  the  outside, 
would  be  dressed  before  the  heat  penetrated  the  mass;  the 
juice  being  thus  shut  in,  the  joint  would  be  under  dressed. 
Some  persons  prefer  meat  roasted  very  slowly.  That  method 
is  expensive  because  it  requires  a  large  fire  to  be  kept  up 
for  a  length  of  time,  and  also,  unless  done  by  a  cook  who 
understands  his  business  well,  and  who  makes  a  fire  for  it, 
the  meat  is  apt  to*  get  sodden.  We  need  scarcely  say  that 
the  meat  screen  should  be  placed  behind  it  from  the  first 
of  its  being  put  down.  Sometimes  the  oven  it  too  hot, 
cover  the  meat  with  a  piece  of  paper,  baste  the  meat  very 
frequently,  for  the  more  it  is  basted  the  better  it  will  taste. 
When  it  is  nearly  done,  the  paper  over  it  should  be  removed 
and  the  joint  lightly  dredged  with  flour,  in  order  to  give  it 
a  savory  brown  appearance  called  frothing.  Sprinkle  a  very 
little  salt  on  it  also,  but  not  till  it  is  just  ready  to  be  dished 
up,  as  salt  draws  out  the  gravy. 

The  usual  time  allowed  for  roasting  is  a  quarter  of  an 
hour  of  twenty  minutes,  according  as  it  is  desired  rare  or 
vtell  done,  for  each  pound  of  meat.  But  the  rule  does  not  ah 
ways  answer.  Meat  freshly  killed  takes  longer  to  roast  than 
when  it  has  been  kept  long;  and  in  warm  weather  it  takes  less 
time  than  in  cold.  Brown  meat  requires  less  time  than  white 
meat  does.  In  frosty  weather  it  is  better  to  lay  the  joint 
before  the  fire  to  thaw  before  it  is  put  on  the  spit  or  in  the 
oven,  as,  if  frozen,  it  will  be  impossible  to  calculate  the 
time  required  for  dressing  it,  and,  in  fact,  it  will  never  be 
dressed  through.  The  cook  should  always  be  careful  of 
the  spit  or  the  oven,  and  also  of  the  fork  used  for  turning 

—  461  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


the  roast  meat,  so  as  to  avoid  disfiguring  it  by  running  the 
spit  through  the  prime  parts. 

Spits  are  the  best  for  large  kitchens;  for  small  families, 
the  range  or  gas  oven,  Dutch  oven  or  the  bottleqack  in  a 
tinned  screen  does  very  well,  or  better  still,  the  improved 
spring'jack  and  roaster. 

When  the  roast  meat  is  taken  up,  the  fat  which  has 
dripped  from  it  into  the  pan  should  be  poured  into  a  basin, 
previously  dipped  in  cold  water.  It  must  be  left  till  the 
next  day,  when  beneath  the  fat  at  the  top  will  be  found 
a  fine  meat  jelly  fit  for  gravies,  etc. 

Veal,  pork  and  lamb  should  be  “thoroughly”  done,  so  as 
not  to  retain  any  red  gravy;  at  the  same  time  care  should  be 
.  taken  not  to  dry  them  up,  or  roast  them  till  the  flesh  parts 
from  the  bones. 

Mutton  does  not  take  quite  the  length  of  time  to  roast 
that  beef  does.  A  very  economical  way  of  making  gravy  is 
to  skim  the  fat  from  the  dripping  in  the  pan  under  the 
meat,  and  pour  two  or  .three  spoonfuls  of  hot  water  into  and 
it  and  pour  it  over  the  meat  through  a  sieve. 

Time  required  for  divers  roasting  meat:  See  article  on 
meat. 

PLAIN  DIRECTIONS  HOW  TO  BROIL 

Many  kinds  of  fish,  steaks,  chops  and  cutlets  are  far 
better  broiled  than  fried;  and  the  charcoal  broiler  is  superior 
to  any  gas  or  electric  broiler,  but  much  care,  science  and 
skill  are  required  to  broil  properly.  First,  the  fire  should 
be  perfectly  free  from  smoke,  though  brisk,  and  giving  out 
a  good  heat;  secondly,  the  gridiron  should  be  scrupulously 
clean,  well  heated,  and  rubbed  over  with  mutton  suet  or 
pork  rind  before  the  meat  is  put  on  it.  If  the  fire  be  too 
fierce,  the  meat  will  be  hardened  and  scorched;  if  it  be  too 
dead,  the  gravy  will  escape  and  the  meat  will  be  flabby. 
The  gridiron  should  be  held  slopingly  over  fire  in  order 
that  the  fat  may  run  off  to  the  back  of  the  grate,  for  if 
it  dropped  on  the  charcoal  it  would  create  a  blase,  and 
blacken  and  smoke  the  meat  .  If  by  chance  a  blaze  should 
spring  up  during  the  time  the  steak,  etc.,  is  broiling,  the 
gridiron  must  be  caught  off  the  fire  and  held  to  one  side 
till  the  blaze  is  gone;  a  little  salt  thrown  on  the  fire  will 
make  it  clear  again.  The  best  way,  however,  is  to  put  any 
articles  on  hot  gridiron  only  after  they  have  first  been 

—  462  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


dipped  in  a  clarified  butter  or  good  olive  oil.  To  season, 
salt  and  pepper.  Steak  and  chops  should  be  turned  often 
in  order  that  they  may  be  done  in  every  part,  according  to 
your  like — rare,  medium  or  well  done — but  the  fork  used 
for  this  purpose  should  never  be  stuck  into  the  lean  of  the 
steak,  but  it  should  be  put  into  the  outer  skin  or  fat.  All 
kitchens  ought  to  be  provided  with  steak  tongs  for  this 
purpose. 

Birds,  when  cut  asunder  and  broiled,  must  be  laid  with 
the  “inside”  first  to  the  fire. 

Most  people  prefer  broiled  lamb  chops  or  beefsteak 
rather  lightly  dressed,  but  veal  and  pork  chops  should  be 
thoroughly  cooked.  Everything  broiled  should  be  served 
the  moment  it  is  done’  very  hot.  The  dish  should  be  kept 
ready  to  receive  it  in  front  of  the  fire.  Broiled  breaded 
chops,  cutlet  or  divers  meats,  must  be  seasoned,  dipped  in 
clarified  butter  before  breaded.  Great  care  should  be  used  tak¬ 
ing  them  off  the  gridiron.  When  fish  are  broiled,  dip  in  oil  or 
clarified  butter  before  placing  on  the  hot  gridiron.  Before 
putting  on,  rub  gridiron  bars  with  bacon  rind.  A  cinder  or 
charcoal  fire  is  best  for  broiling  fish. 

While  you  are  broiling  slices  of  cold  meat  put  into  a 
hot  dish  a  piece  of  butter,  a  teaspoonful  of  ketchup  and 
Worcestershire  sauce,  seasoning,  melt  them  together;  add 
mustard  if  you  like,  and  lay  the  meat  sprinkled  with  fresh 
bread  crumbs,  on  the  gridiron.  Serve  separate  sauce — De¬ 
viled,  Robert’s,  Piquante,  etc. 

BONING,  LARDING  AND  BRAISING 

The  three  most  difficult  operations  to  achieve  well  in 
'cookery  are  braising,  boning  and  larding.  Boning  is  so 
little  understood  by  inferior  cooks  (man  or  woman)  that  it 
is  best,  if  your  servant  is  not  first-rate,  to  have  it  done  by 
the  poulterer  with  whom  you  deal.  Nevertheless,  it  is  an 
art  which  tends  so  much  to  economy  that  it  would  quite 
repay  the  mistress  of  a  family  to  pay  for  a  few  lessons  for 
her  domestic  from  a  good  poulterer  or  first-class  Chef  or 
cook.  The  bones  of  poultry  and  hares  are  most  useful  for 
making  gravies,  and  hares  are  more  easily  carved,  and  look 
better  when  boned.  Any  butcher  will  bone  joints  when 
required.  Although  we  can  not  hope  that  our  readers  will 
be  able  to  achieve  the  boning  of  a  chicken,  turkey,  game, 

—  463  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


etc.,  from  any  verbal  description,  we,  nevertheless  give  a 
few  directions  on  the  subject,  from  an  excellent  demonstra< 
tion,  and  practicing  for  many  performances  and  operations. 
(Use  only  strictly  dry  pick  poultry.) 

To  bone  chicken,  turkey,  etc.,  “cut  through  the  skin 
down  the  center  of  the  back,  and  raise  the  ilesh,  carefully 
on  either  side;  inject  the  point  of  a  sharp  small  knife  until 
the  socket  of  the  wings  and  thighs  are  reached.  Until  a  little 
practice  has  been  gained,  it  will,  perhaps,  be  better  to  bone 
these  joints  before  proceeding  further;  but  after  they  are 
once  detached  from  it  the  whole  of  the  body  may  easily  be 
separated  from  the  flesh  and  taken  out  entire.  Only  the 
neck  bone  and  merry  thought  will  then  remain  to  be  re" 
moved.  The  bird  thils  prepared  may  either  be  restored  to 
its  original  form,  by  filling  the  body  with  chicken  forcemeat, 
goose  liver,  rice  or  wild  rice  mixed  with  goose  liver,  green 
corn,  etc.,  or  galantine  style,  and  the  bird  being  first  flat" 
tened  on  a  table  may  be  covered  with  sausage  meat  and  the 
various  other  ingredients  we  have  named,  so  placed  that  it 
shall  be  of  equal  thickness  in  every  part,  then  tightly  rolled, 
bound  firmly  together  with  a  filet  of  broad  tape,  wrapped 
in  a  thin  fine  cheese  cloth  closely  tied  at  both  ends  and 
dressed. 

For  roasting  boned  chicken,  etc.,  put  in  roast  pan  covered 
with  melted  butter;  cook  in  medium  oven  according  to  the 
size  of  bird.  (See  article  for  roasting.) 

HOW  TO  LARD 

The  cook  should  be  provided  with  larding  needles  of 
various  sizes.  Cut  small,  smooth  strips  of  the  length  re" 
quired  of  the  firmest  part  of  a  piece  of  larding  pork.  Put 
these  bits  of  lardons  into  a  larding  needle.  Pierce  the  skin 
and  a  very  little  of  the  flesh  of  the  meat,  using  filet  of  beef, 
sweetbread,  chicken,  turkey,  partridge,  quail,  pheasant,  etc., 
you  may  wish  to  lard  with  it,  having  the  lardons  in,  and  the 
two  ends  of  equal  length  outwards.  These  punctures  for 
lardons  are  made  in  rows  at  any  distance  from  each  other 
the  cook  pleases.  The  flavor  of  larding  may  be  obtained 
by  raising  the  skin  of  the  meat  and  laying  a  slice  of  fat 
lardons  beneath  it;  this  move  is  not  ornamental,  but  gives  an 
excellent  flavor  to  the  flesh,  even  better  than  when  larded  with 
the  needles.  It  requires  a  little  practice  to  lard  neatly,  but 

—  464  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


as  it  is-  really  an  easy  operation,  any  cook  may  learn  to  do  it 
with  care.  Cut  the  larding  pork  in  slices,  lay  them  one  on 
the  other  (according  to  size),  and  cut  strips  through  them 
the  sise  you  require,  in  order  that  they  may  be  all  the  same 
.size.  (Called  in  French,  barde.)  Cover  with  larding  pork. 

Lardons  should  be  two  inches  in  length  and  one^eighth 
of  an  inch  in  width  for  larding  poultry,  game,  etc.;  for 
filets  of  beef,  loin  of  veal  or  fricandeau  (kernel  of  veal) 
they  should  be  rather  thicker.  We  owe,  besides  many 
another  invaluable  lesson,  the  following  admirable  description 
of  larding  to  Escoffier,  Urbain  Dubois,  Garlin,  etc.  (Any 
good  cook  book.) 

“Have  the  fricandeau  trimmed;  lay  it  lengthwise  upon  a 
clean  cloth  across  your  hand,  forming  a  damn  with  your 
thumb  at  the  part  you  are  about  to  commence  at.  Then 
with  the  point  of  the  larding  needle  make  three  distinct  lines 
across,  half  an  inch  apart;  run  the  needle  into  the  third  line 
at  the  further  side  of  the  fricandeau  and  bring  it  out  at  the 
first,  placing  one  of  the  lardons  in  it;  draw  the  needle 
through,  leaving  out  a  quarter  of  an  inch  of  the  lardons  at 
each  line.  Proceed  then  to  the  end  of  the  row.  Then  make 
another  line  half  an  inch  distant;  stick  in  another  row  of 
lardons,  bringing  them  out  at  the  second  line,  leaving  the 
ends  of  the  lardons  out  all  the  same  length.  Make  the  next 
row  again  at  the  same  distance,  bringing  the  ends  out  be< 
tween  the  lardons  of  the  first  row,  proceeding  in  this  manner 
until  the  whole  surface  is  larded  in  chequered  rows.  Every' 
thing  else  is  larded  in  a  similar  way,  and  in  the  case  of 
poultry,  hold  the  breast  over  fire  for  one  minute,  or  dip 
it  into  boiling  water,  in  order  to  make  the  flesh  firm.” 

Note:  Sometimes  lardons  are  mixed  with  smoked  tongue 
or  truffle  for  fancy  pieces  or  decorations,  according  to  the 
taste. 

HOW  TO  FRY 

Cooks  should  always  have  a  few  frying  pans,  and  a  third, 
not  much  larger  than  a  large  plate,  for  omelettes,  fritters, 
etc.,  if  they  have  no  saute'pan.  The  pan  must  be  kept 
delicately  clean  and  nice;  the  butter,  dripping  lard  or  oil  in 
which  the  fish,  meat,  etc.,  is  fried,  must  always  be  “boiling 
hot”  before  the  meat  is  put  into  the  pan.  The  rule  is  that 
a  sufficient  quantity  of  fat  must  be  heated  thus  in  the  pan 

—  465  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


“to  cover”  the  steak,  chop,  or  whatever  is  to  be  fried,  frying 
being  actually  boiling  in  fat  instead  of  water.  Mutton-chops 
or  lamb  do  not  require  any  fat  in  the  pan  with  them;  they 
have  enough  in  themselves,  but  they  must  be  often  turned 
and  moved  about  to  prevent  them  from  burning.  Of  course, 
we  speak  only  of  chops  cooked  quite  plain  i.  e.,  without 
being  egged  and  bread-crumbed.  Cut  and  skin  the  chop 
nicely,  and  season  it  with  a  little  pepper  before  putting  it 
in  the  pan. 

Lamb  chops,  lamb  cutlet  or  veal,  must  be  egged  and 
bread-crumbed  twice  in  order  to  look  well.  Steaks  should  be 
cut  three-quarters  of  an  inch  thick  for  frying,  and  should  be 
peppered,  but  not  put  on  them  before  they  are  dressed,  as 
it  makes  them  hard.  When  done,  a  little  salt  is  sprinkled 
lightly  over  them. 

■The  first  process  in  frying  is  to  put  enough  dripping  or 
butter  in  your  pan  to  cover  the  chop  or  steak  when  the  butter 
is  melted.  Then  the  fast  must  be  made  to  “boil”  in  the 
pan,  and  when  at  its  greatest  heat  the  substance  to  be  fried 
must  be  plunged  into  it.  The  pan  must  then  be  lifted  from 
the  fire  for  a  minute  or  two,  to  prevent  the  outside  from 
getting  black  before  the  inside  is  dressed. 

Fish  must  be  well  dried  before  frying,  or  saute  in  butter; 
should  be  seasoned  in  milk  and  floured;  or  first  they  may 
be  wiped  well,  thoroughly  dried  and  dredged  with  flour.  Then 
an  egg  is  well  brushed  over  them,  and  finely-grated  bread  or 
cracker  dust  and  flour.  The  fat  should  be  “quite”  at  boiling- 
point  (when  it  will  no  longer  hiss  or  bubble),  before  the  fish 
is  put  in,  and  it  should  be  well  covered  by  the  liquid  butter, 
oil  or  lard,  which,  by-the-bye,  is  much  the  best  for  frying 
fish  in,  but  of  course  it  is  expensive.  Hog’s  lard  and  drip¬ 
ping  are  also  used  in  economic  kitchens,  but  the  fish  is  too 
soft  after  frying.  The  frying  pan  should  never  be  left  for  a 
moment  until  the  fish  is  done. 

In  kitchens  where  strict  economy  is  demanded,  it  is  usual, 
when  liver  and  bacon  are  to  be  dressed,  to  fry  the  bacon 
“first,”  which  will  leave  enough  fat  in  the  pan  for  the  liver 
to  be  put  in  without  either  butter  or  dripping. 

BRAISING  POELER  OR  POGHE 

Braising  all  kinds  of  meat  and  poultry  cooked  in  a  brais- 
ing-pan,  Poeler  or  Poche  are  ancient  terms  used  as  modes  of 

—  466  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


braising.  “Poche”  is  a  French  term  used  for  poached.  All 
poultry  white  (hot  or  cold).  All  kinds  of  poultry  or  meat 
cooked  in  braising'pan,  with  sliced  onions,  carrots,  celery, 
spices  and  condiments,  rind  of  pork  on  the  bottom  of  brais' 
ing'pan.  First,  meat  or  poultry  on  top;  let  it  get  browned, 
smothered  and  moistened  with  wine  or  stock,  according  to 
meat,  of  cooking  by  the  action  of  heat.  A  braising'pan  is 
air-tight,  and  as  all  evaporation  is  thus  precluded,  the  food 
braised  imbibes  whatever  flavor  the  cook  may  wish  to  give 
it;  in  order  to  effect  this,  she  must  place  in  the  pan  with 
it  whatever  vegetables,  etc.,  her  recipe  may  direct.  The  ingre' 
dients  should  be  very  well  proportioned  and  the  stewing 
should  go  on  very  slowly.  We  will  give  here  one  recipe  for 
a  braise,  as  an  example  of  what  is  meant: 

Lay  a  few  slices  of  fat  of  larding  pork  on  the  bottom  of 
the  braising'pan,  place  the  chicken  trussed  for  braising,  or 
meat,  on  it.  Lay  the  vegetables  around  it,  with  a  few  bones 
or  trimmings  of  fresh  meat.  Cover  the  braising'pan  closely, 
set  it  over  a  slow  fire  or  in  the  oven,  or  on  top  of  the 
range.  After  twenty  minutes,  add  one  pint  or  more  of  wine 
or  stock,  seasoning  it  to  your  taste,  and  let  it  cook  slowly, 
according  to  the  size  of  chicken  or  your  meat. 

When  it  is  done  keep  the  meat  hot  while  you  strain  the 
gravy  and  take  off  the  fat,  which  you  can  do  quicker  by 
plunging  the  basin  partly  into  cold  water  or  ice;  this  makes 
the  fat  coagulate.  Boil  it  up  very  quickly  again  until  it 
thickens.  Some  cooks  let  the  gravy  adhere  to  the  meat; 
this  is  done  by  boiling  it  down  until  it  is  reduced  to  just  the 
quantity  required  for  the  purpose. 

Note:  The  ingredients  should  be  very  well  proportioned, 
onions,  carrots,  turnips,  celery,  baydeaves,  thyme,  etc.,  peeled 
and  washed  and  the  stewing  should  go  very  slowly. 

HOW  TO  BOIL 

Joints  to  be  boiled  should  be  washed  extremely  clean  and 
skewered  into  good  shape;  then  they  should  be  put  in  the 
saucepan  and  covered  well  with  water  or  stock.  They  must 
be  set  over  a  moderate  fire  and  let  boil  “slowly.”  Just  before 
the  water  reaches  boiling  point  the  scum  will  rise  to  the  top, 
and  must  be  carefully  skimmed  off;  if  not  done  at  the  moment 
of  ebulition  it  will  fall  back  on  the  joint  boiling  and  disfigure 

—  467  — 


G  ANCEL’S  C  U  LIN  ARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


it.  The  pot  will  require  skimming  every  time  the  scum  rises'; 
the  saucepan  must  be  kept  covered  all  the  time,  however, 
the  lid  ,  being  only  removed  for  the  cook  to  skim  the  pot. 

“Gentle:  simmering,”  not  fast  boiling,  is  most  desirable 
for  meat,  as  by  quick  boiling  the  outside  is  hardened  before 
the  joint  is  done,  and  the  meat  becomes  hard  and  tough, 
from  the  chemical  reasons  already  given. 

Salted  meat  requires  longer  boiling  than  fresh  .meat; 
when  smoked  and  dried,  it  takes  longer  still.  Pickled  or 
salted  meat  should  be  soaked  before  boiling  in  cold  Water 
for  a  longer  or  shorter  time,  as  its  saltiness  and  size  may 
require.  Take  care  that  the  joint,  if  large,  does  not  adhere 
to  the  bottom  of  the  pot;  to  prevent  this  possibility  cooks 
sometimes  put  a  few  wooden  skewers  at  the  bottom  Under  it. 
The  time  allowed  for  boiling  is  from  a  quarter  of  an  hour  to 
twenty  minutes  for  each  pound.  (1)  Supposing,  of  cPursq, 
that  the  fire  is  kept  up;  (2)  “see  article  roasting  meat;’’  to 
an  equal  temperature  all  the  time.  Quick  boiling  is  very 
much  to  be  avoided,  but  the  pot  should  never  be  allowed 
“to  stop  simmering.”  First-rate  cooks  preserve  the  whiteness 
of  their  boiled  meats,  and  save  them  from  insipidity  by 
“not”  boiling  them  in  water,  but  using  instead  a  sort  of 
broth  called  in  French  “poele,”  or  another  called  “a  blanc.” 
But  these  preparations  are  very  expensive,  and  are  not 
required  for  ordinary  use;  however,  we  give  them  amongst 
the  sauces  in  case  they  should  be  needed.  For  people  who 
cannot  afford  expensive  cooking,  a  well-floured  cloth  wrap¬ 
ped  round  the  meat  to  be  boiled  will  make  it  white;  but  the 
cloth  must  be  kept  very  clean,  and  should  be  boiled  in  pure 
water  after  each  time  it  is  used;  moreover,  it  must  not  be 
suffered  to  get  damp,  or  it  will  give  a  musty  flavor  to  the 
meat. 

HOW  TO  STEW 

Stewing  is  a  wholesome  and  excellent  and  economical 
mode  of  cooking.  Very  little  fuel  is  used  for  it,  and  meat  so 
prepared  is  both  digestible  and  delicious.  But  “boiling”  is 
not  “stewing;”  and  we  warn  our  readers  that  all  we  have 
said  in  praise  of  it  may  be  reversed  if  they  let  the  stew¬ 
ing  pan  do  more  than  simmer  very  gently.  Stewing  is  best 
done  over  a  regular  stove;  but  when  a  cook  can  command 
only  an  old-fashioned  kitchen  range  she  must  place  her  stew- 


—  468  — 


CULINARY  E  V  O  L U  TION 


pan  on  trivets  high  above  the.  fire,  and  constantly  watch 
it  and  move  it  nearer  to,  or  further  from  the  fire.  Stewing 
must  of  course  always  be  done  over  slow  fire  and  the  stew- 
pan  lid  should  shut  quite'  closely.  It  should  be  kept  at  a 
gentle  simmer,  without  letting  it  boil,  and  it  must  stew  for 
several  hours,  according  to  the  weight  of  the  meat,  which  is 
not  to  be  considered  done  until  it  is  quite  tender.  Sometimes 
the  cook  stews  the  meat  in  the  earthen  pot  (the  mode  once 
Used  in  southern  France),  this  is  very  good,  placed  in  a 
Stew-pan  full  of  water,  and  thus  extracts,  the  pure  gravy 
unmixed  with  water.  We  have,  also,  a  recipe  for  stewing 
meat  and  vegetables  together,  without  water  being  put  in  the 
earthen  pot  with  them,  thus  making  an  excellent  soup,  from 
the  union  of  the  pieces  of  the  meat  or  chicken  and  the  water 
contained  in  the  vegetables.  Call  it  French  Pot-au-feu,  Ga- 
bure,  etc. 

HOW  TO  SAUTE 

Saute,  a  French  term — (Cooked  quickly  over  a  sharp 
fire).  To  “saute”  anything  means  to  dress  it  quickly,  in  a 
small  pan,  with  a  “very  little”  butter,  oil,  lard,  or  dripping, 
doing  one  side  at  a  time.  Two  spoonfuls  of  oil  will  be 
enough  to  “saute”  a  small  chicken  in. 

The  art  of  saute-ing  well  consists  of  doing  it  quickly,  to 
keep  the  gravy  and  succulence  in  the  meat.  It  is  an  econom¬ 
ical  mode  of  dressing  small  things  of  every  kind  of  food. 
It  is,  you  see,  very  different  from  frying,  which  is  really 
boiling  in  hot  fat,  and  which  requires  far  greater  quantity  of 
the  butter,  oil,  etc.,  for  its  performance. 

Carved  Chicken  Saute.  Take  one  or  two  fine  spring 
chickens,  and  after  plucking  and  drawing  them,  cut  them 
in  pieces;  take  off  the  wing  and  leg  on  one  side;  then  the 
wing  and  leg  on  the  other.  Take  off  the  breast  and  cut 
it  in  halves  (cross-wise,  not  length-wise);  cut  the  back  also 
in  three  parts.  Cut  off  the  neck,  separate  the  pinions  from 
the  wing.  Divide  the  thigh  from  the  drumstick,  scald  the 
feed  and  take  off  the  skin.  Cut  the  nails  of  the  feet  off, 
clean  the  head  and  eyes  off,  cut  off  the  beak.  Season:  Put 
some  very  good  butter  or  olive  oil  in  a  frying  pan,  Keep 
turning  until  well  browned,  when  cooked  add  garnishings 
you  desire.  Do  not  let  cook  one  or  two  minutes  longer  in 
the  sauce,  as  it  becomes  hard.  Time:  fifteen  minutes. 

—  469  — 


CANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENC  YCLOPEDIA 

STEAMING 

Steaming  is  an  admirable  mode  of  dressing  corned  beef, 
hams,  bacon,  etc.  It  preserves  the  flavor  in  them  and  quite 
saves  waste.  Anyone  who  has  once  eaten  steamed  bacon, 
etc.,  will  never  again,  we  think,  order  it  to  be  boiled,  so 
tender  and  succulent  will  it  be  found. 

In  it  the  meat  or  poultry  is  cooked  without  being  touched 
by  either  water  or  steam.  It  appears  to  us1  to  be  a  cheap 
and  easy  mode  of  braising  or  boiling  in  a  new  form. 

It  is  styled  “Everybody’s  Cooking  Pot.’’  The  novelty  of 
the  process  consists  in  cooking  the  viands  without  coming 
into  actual  contact  with  water  or  steam.  The  meat,,  kept 
from  water  entirely,  is  cooked  in  an  inner  cylinder,  the  outer 
one  containing  the  water  being  kept  at  boiling-point.  The 
food  thus  prepared  is  cooked  in  its  own  vapors  and  none 
of  its  nutritious  properties  wasted. 

The  well-known  fact  that  meat,  cooked  by  the  ordinary 
methods  of  roasting  or  boiling,  loses  a  large  portion  both  in 
bulk  and  weight,  as  well  as  some  very  important  chemical 
qualities,  is  of  itself  sufficient  to  stamp  with  the  seal  of 
approbation  any  invention  that  avoids  these  evils.  We  have 
proved  by  personally  trying  it,  that  meat,  fish  and  poultry, 
when  cooked  in  this  pot,  retain  those  nourishing  juices  which, 
if  cooked  in  the  ordinary  method,  would  have  been  thrown 
off  in  vapor,  but  by  this  mode  become  condensed  and  are 
returned  in  moisture  at  a  temperature  sufficient  to  cook  ip 
the  most  perfect  manner.  To  the  poor  as  well  as  the  rich, 
this  invention  will  therefore  prove  a  great  boon. 

Note:  I  was  using  the  first  steam  pot  (marmite  a  vapeur) 
in  1878.  Paris  Exhibition.  Invented  in  Paris  by  Noel 
Peters,  founder  of  the  Restaurants  Peters,  passage  des  Prim 
ces.  and  Cafe  Americain,  boulevard  des  Italiens;  all  large 
hotels  or  restaurants  today  use  the  large  steam  kettle.  ; 

HOW  TO  CARVE 

One  of  the  most  important  acquisitions  in  the  routine 
of  daily  life  is  the  ability  to  carve,  not  only  well,  but 
elegantly,  r, i  ,  ■  ,-'r-  ,  •.■•v  .^7 

It  is  true  that  the  present  fashion  of  Russian  dinners  is 
fast  banishing  the  necessity  for  promiscuous  carving  from 
the  richly-served  boards  of  the  wealthy;  but  in  the  circles  of 

—  470  — 


CULINARY  E  V  O  L  U  T  I  O  N 


middle  life,  where  it  is  not  adopted,  the  necessity  of  skill  in 
the  use  of  a  carving-knife  is  sufficiently  obvious. 

Ladies  ought  especially  to  make  carving  a  study;  at 
their  own  houses,  and  at  the  family  dinner,  they  should  be 
enabled  to  perform  the  task  allotted  to  them  with  sufficient 
skill  to  prevent  remark,  or  the  calling  forth  of  eager  proffers 
of  assistance  from  good-natured  visitors  near,  who  probably 
would  far  rather  not  be  so  employed. 

Carving  presents  no  real  difficulties;  it  is  simply  know¬ 
ledge.  All  displays  of  exertion  or  violence  are  in  very  bad 
taste;  for  if  they  are  not  evidence  of  the  want  of  ability  on 
the  part  of  the  carver,  they  present  a  very  strong  testimony 
of  the  toughness  of  a  joint  or  the  more  than  full  age  of  a 
bird;  in  both  cases  they  should  be  avoided.  A  good  knife  of 
moderate  size,  sufficient  length  of  handle  and  very  sharp,  is 
a  requisite.  The  carving-knife  for  a  lady  should  be  light  and 
smaller  than  that  used  by  gentlemen. 

Poultry  are  very  easily  carved;  and  of  joints,  such  as  loins, 
breasts,  fore-quarters,  etc.,  the  butcher  should  have  strict 
injunctions  to  separate  the  joints.  Successful  carving  requires 
good  tools,  will  and  a  knowledge  of  how  to  carve.  Skill 
depends  upon  the  individual;  the  good  tools  are  these:  One 
sharp-bladed  knife,  one  fork  (two  or  three  pronged),  one 
pair  carving  scissors  (used  only  in  the  kitchen),  two  serving 
spoons,  one  with  a  long  handle  for  reaching  stuffings. 

Here  are  a  few  points  usually  observed:  (1)  Meat-to.be 
Carved  should  be  conveniently  near  the  person  carving  it. 
(2)  It  should  be  placed  on  a  dish  sufficiently  large  so  the 
dish  will  not  have  to  be  moved.  The  carving  dish  should 
be  sufficiently  deep,  so  the  meat  will  not  slip  in  it.  (3)  Beef, 
veal,  lamb,  mutton,  pork  and  ham  are  sliced  thin,  and  as  a 
general  rule,  the  article  to  be  carved  is  placed  conveniently 
near  to  the  carver,  so  that  he  may  have  full  control  over  it. 
For  if  it  is  placed  far  off,  nothing  can  prevent  an  ungrace- 
fulness  of  appearance  and  a  difficulty  in  performing  that 
which,  if  it  were  in  its  proper  place,  could  be  achieved  with 
ease.  . 

In  the  foregoing  directions,  we  have  endeavored  to  be 
as  explicit  as  possible;  but  practice  alone  will  enable  any 
person  to  carve  with  skill  and  facility. 

Ribs  of  Beef.  There  are  two  modes'  of  carving  this  joint; 
the  first,  which  is  now  becoming  common,  and  is  .  easy  to 

—  471  — 


G  ANGEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


an  amateur,,  carver,;  is  to  cut  across  the  bone’,  commencing  in 
the  center,  and  helping  fat,  or  it  should  be  carved  in 
slices,  commencing  either  in  the  center  of  the  joint  or  at  the 
sides.  Occasionally  the  bones  are  removed,  and  the  meat 
formed-  into  a'  fillet.  It  should  then  be  carved  as  a  round  of 
beef.’-.;  3  gss*  1$  fteo’t  3§ni  ^  .  f&qte?  tosyoiq  ot  iMa 

Sirloin  of  Beef.  The  under  part  should  be  first  carved, 
as  indicated  for  the  ribs,  carving  either  side1,  or  in  the  center, 
and  helping  the  fat.  f  r 

Rack  or  Loin  Veal.  Veal  is  not  a  nourishing  food,  biit 
is  eaten  considerably.  It  must  be  of  first  quality,  firm,  white 
and  elastic,  and  at  least  six  weeks  old.  4* 

Were  you  to  attempt  to  carve  each  chop,  and  serve  it, 
you  would  not  only  place  a  gigantic  piece  upon  the  plate 
of  the  person,  you  intended  to  help,  but  you  woqld  waste 
much  .time,  and  should  the  vertebrea  not  have  been  jointed 
by  the  butcher,  you  would  find  yourself  in  the  position  of 
the  ungraceful  carver,  being  compelled  to  exercise  a  degree 
of  strength  which  should  never  be  suffered  to  appear;  Very 
possibly,  too,  helping  gravy  in  a  manner  not  contemplated 
by.  the  person  unfortunately  near  enough  to  receive  it,  but 
diagonally,  and  help  in  slices  of  moderate  thickness.  Separate 
small  bones,  divide  and  serve  them,  having  first  inquired  if 
they  are  desired,  -  V  * 

Loin  of  Veal.  This  joint  is  sent  to  the  table  served  as  a 
sirloin  of  beef.  Having  turned  it  over,  cut  out  the  kidney 
and  the  fat,  return  it  to  its  proper  position,  and  carve  it  as 
in  the  neck  of  veal;  help  with  it  a  slice  of  kidney  and  fat; 
loirir  and  rack  of  a  nice  part,  after  leg  and  shoulder  of  veal. 

Lamb  and  Mutton.  Lamb  and  mutton  are  the  best 
meats  after  beef.'  Lamb  after  age  of  six  months,  is  called 
mutton.  The  meat  of  the  lamb  before  two  months  has 
little  flavor;  is  sticky,  white  and  soft.  After  that  time  it  is 
delicious,  especially  if  the  animal  has  been  fed  upon  milk, 
the  meat  being  tender  and  very  nourishing  for  children, 
convalascents  and  old,  feeble  people.  , 

Saddle  of  lamb  or  mutton  and  chops,  is  the  best  roasted 
or  broiled  after  the  leg  and  shoulder.  The  preparation  of 
mutton  is  the  same  as  for  lamb,  and  it  is  eaten  rare,  but 
lamb  is  eaten  well  cooked.  ” 

1  ^  \  IjL  •  •  <  r  ■  '■  ,  >  •  •  n  .  •  •  . .  ’  4  .  .  , 

Saddle  of  Lamb  or  Mutton.  The  tail  end  is  divided,  and 

472  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


the  kidneys  skewered  under  each  division;  this  is  a  matter 
of  taste,  and  is  not  always  done.  Carve  in  thin  slices;  cut 
English  style  is  lengthwise.  You  may  help  from  the  vertebrea 
on  both  sides  of  the  loin,  and  then  carve  cross-wise,  which 
gives  you  both  fat  and  lean;  help  a  slice  of  kidney  to  those 
who  desire  it.  The  loin  should  be  carved  in  chops,  with  the 
outer  chop  or  English  chop. 

Leg  of  Lamb  or  Mutton.  The  under  or  thickest  part  of 
the  leg  should  be  placed  uppermost,  and  carved  in  slices 
moderately  thin.  Many  persons  prefer  the  knuckle,  and  this 
question  should  be  asked,  and  if  liked,  it  should  be  served 
to  the  guest.  When  cold,  the  back  of  the  leg  should  be 
placed  uppermost,  and  thus  carved;  serve  the  cramp  bone  if 
requested  (some  persons  regard  it  as  a  dainty). 

The  shoulder,  breast  and  neck,  cut  into  small,  square 
pieces,  is  used  for  stew. 

Divers  Pork.  As  there  are  so  many  different  styles  of 
carving  pork,  it  is  far  better  to  go  to  a  first-class  pork  butcher 
than  to  prepare  it  one’s  self,  and  get  your  choice,  such  as 
ham,  bacon,  salt  pork,  larding  pork,  sausages,  etc.  With¬ 
out  pork  we  would  have  no  lard,  and  cooking  lard  is  just  as 
indispensable  as  butter  in  the  kitchen.  Also,  the  same  manner 
of  carving  as  big  meat,  racks  or  loin,  leg  or  shoulder,  etc. 
Smoked  ham  and  bacon,  jaw,  shoulder,  head,  rack,  etc. 

Suckling  pig  is  a  young  one  of  six  or  eight  weeks,  which 
has  been  nourished  with  milk.  The  suckling  pig  is  served 
whole  roasted  with  divers  stuffing — English  style,  French 
style,  American  style,  German  style.  The  cook  should  send 
a  roast  pig  to  the  table  garnished  with  head  and  ears;  carve 
the  joints,  divide  the  ribs,  serve  with  plenty  of  sauce.  Should 
one  of  the  joints  be  too  much,  it  may  separated.  Stuffing 
should  accompany  it.  An  ear  and  the  jaw  are  .favorite 
parts  with  many  people.  The  suckling  pig  is  served  whole, 
roasted,  cold,  boneless,  and  cut  in  small  pieces,  cooked  with 
stock  and  aromate,  served  cold  with  natural  jelly. 

Large  and  Small  Game.  Large  game — “Venison” — is 
composed  of  deer,  reindeer,  elk,  chamois,  wild  boar,  ante¬ 
lope,  etc.  Small  game  is  composed  of  hare,  rabbit,  raccoon, 
squirrel,  opossum,  etc. 

Game  should  be  cooked  and  served  instantly  off  the  fire. 
All  dark  meat  is  served  rare;  white  meat  well  done,  or 
according  to  desire.  (There  are  three  different  ways  of 

’ —  473  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


cooking  game — roast,  grilled  and  sometimes  saute.  Let  mon 
tify  for  two  or  three  days  before  cooking— so  the  epicure 
says.)  Haunch  of  Venison  is  carved  as  leg  or  saddle  of 
lamb  or  mutton. 

Small  Game.  Hare,  rabbit,  etc.,  are  carved  in  the  same 
manner. 

Cut  slices  of  moderate  thickness.  When  the  hare  is 
young  you  can,  after  removing  the  shoulders  and  legs,  cut 
across  the  back  and  divide  it  into  several  pieces.  This  is 
not  practicable  in  a  full'grown  hare,  unless  it  is  boned.  The 
shoulders  and  legs  are  easily  removed  by  placing  the  knife  be' 
tween  them  and  turning  them  back.  The  joint  will  disclose 
itself  and  can  then  be  separated  .  The  head  should  not  be 
removed  until  the  last.  The  most  tender  part  is  the  loin, 
the  meat  there  being  very  delicate. 

Poultry  requires  skillful  carving.  The  requisites  are  grace 
of  manner,  ease  in  the  performance,  a  perfect  knowledge  of 
the  position  of  the  points,  and  the  most  complete  mode  of 
dissecting,  so  as  to  obtain  the  largest  quantity  of  meat.  In  no 
case  is  this  ability  more  demanded  than  in  carving  a  roast 
turkey.  Unless  this  is  done  well,  there  is  not  only  much 
waste,  but  the  appearance  of  the  turkey  is  spoiled.  You  will 
commence  by  carving  slices  from  each  side  of  the  breast. 
Then  remove  the  legs,  dividing  the  thighs  from  the  drum' 
sticks  (and  here  an  instrument  termed  a  disjointer  will  be 
found  serviceable,  for  unless  the  turkey  is  very  young  and 
the  union  of  the  joints  very  accurately  taken,  carving  becomes 
difficult.  The  disjointer  effects  the  ceparation  at  once  and  it 
possesses  also  the  advantage  of  enabling  the  carver  to  divide 
a  thigh  in  two— thus  permitting  a  less  bulky  portion  to  be 
served.  The  pinions  and  that  portion  of  the  body  removed 
with  it  are  always  a  delicacy,  and  care  should  be  taken  to 
carve  them  nicely. 

Chicken.  Put  a  fork  in  the  wing  and  with  a  knife  cut 
it  off.  In  taking  off  the.  legs,  first  slip  the  knife  between 
the  leg  and  the  body  and  cut,  then  turn  th>s  leg  back  with 
the  fork  and  remove  it.  Later  separate  the  first  and  second 
tooints.  Slice  the  breast,  remove  the  wishbone,  separate  the 
side  bones  and  collar  bone  so  the  back  is  left. 

Pheasant.  Separate  the  leg  by  inserting  the  edge  of  the 
knife  between  it  and  the  body.  Then  take  off  the  wings, 
but  do  not  remove  much  of  the  breast  with  them.  You  are 

—  474  — 


GULIN  A  R  Y  EVOLUTION 


thus  enabled  to  obtain  some  nice  slices.  The  pheasant  is 
then  carved  as  a  roast  chicken.  The  breast  is  first  in  estima' 
tion;  then  the  wings,  and  after  these  the  merry  thought; 
lovers  of  game  perfer  a  leg. 

Partridge.  Separate  the  legs  and  then  divide  the  bird 
into  three  parts,  leaving  each^eg  and  wing,  or  serve  whole; 
also  cut  in  two. 

Quail  and  Small  Birds.  Roasted  or  split  to  be  broiled. 

Wild  Ducks.  The  breast  of  these  ducks  being  the  best 
portion,  it  is  carved  in  slices,  or  one  wing.  All  wild  ducks 
are  roasted  and  eaten  rare,  served  with  currant  jelly,  fried 
hominy  and  wild  rice,  and  press  the  carcass. 

How  to  Carve  Fish.  Fish  should  never  be  touched  with 
a  steel  knife,  because  a  steel  knife  is  said  to  injure  the 
delicate  flavor  of  fish.  Fish  slicers,  or  a  silver  fish  knife 
and  fork  are  used  for  carving  it.  It  requires  more  care  than 
knowledge  to  help  fish — the  principle  thing  being  to  avoid 
breaking  the  flakes. 

Short'grained  fish,  like  salmon,  should  be  cut  crosswise, 
but  long'grained  fish  should  be  cut  lengthwise.  Care  should 
be  taken  not  to  separate  the  flakes.  The  best  flavored  is 
the  Upper  or  thick  part. 

Remember  that  constant  practice  is  required  to  make  a 
good  carver.  With  it  and  a  little  care  and  observation,  it 
will  become  easy,  and  even  pleasant,  for  you  to  carve;  and  will 
greatly  add  to  the  comfort  and  nicety  of  the  home  dinner' 
table. 

HOW  TO  SELECT  PROVISIONS  AT  THE  MARKET 

For  the  cook  or  housekeeper  the  provisions  are  the  foun' 
dation  stone  of  the  kitchen;  but  as  some  skill  is  required  in 
a  purchaser  (if  this  duty  is  to  be  performed  to  advantage), 
we  will  endeavor  to  give  directions  by  which  inexperienced 
cooks  or  housewives  may  be  able  to  choose  good  articles. 

Why?  Because  there  is  no  medicine  in  good  cooking. 

'  All  meats,  chicken,  large  or  small  game,  vegetables,  fruits, 
butter  for  pastry  should  be  fresh  and  of  the  finest  quality. 
There  are  no  four  seasons  today,  as  from  the  beginning  pi 
the  year  to  the  end,  all  products  are  to  be  found  in  the  hot' 
house  or  the  refrigerator. 

First  in  the  list  comes  butcher’s  meat,  of  which  beef  is 

—  475  — 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


considered  the  best  by  the  generality  of  any  people.  An  ox 
should  be  kept  five  or  six  years  before  it  is  killed;  it  is  then 
in  its  prime.  “Ox  beef”  is  the  best.  It  is  a  fine-grained 
meat;  the  lean  of  a  bright  red  color,  intermingled  with 
grains  o,f  fat,  when  it  is  well  fed  , on  good  grain.  The  fat 
should  be  white,  not  yellow,  and  the  suet  also  white  and 
firm.  Beef  should  never  be  lean;  it  is  tough  and  bad  unless 
there  is  a  good  quantity  of  fat.  Heifer- beef  is  paler  than 
ox-beef  and  closer  grained;  the  fat  whiter,  and  the  bones, 
of  course,  smaller.  Bull-beef  is  only  described  to  be  avoided. 
It  is  dark  colored  and  coarse  grained;  has  very  little  fat,  and 
a  strong  meaty  smell  about  it. 

Of  these  joints  choose  the  rib  or  sirloin,  for  roasting.  If 
you  purchase  ribs  of  beef,  let  them  be  the  middle  ribs.  You 
may  have  one,  two,  three  or  four  ribs,  as  you  will;  but  one 
rib  is  too  thin  to  be  economical,  as  it  dries  up  in  cooking. 
If,  however,  your  family  be  small,  a  single  rib  with  the 
bones  taken  out,  are  only  good  for  rib  steak.  If  you  buy  a 
sirloin,  take  care  to  have  it  cut  from  the  chump  end,  which 
has  a  good  under-cut  or  filets,  as  then,  in  addition  to  a  roast 
joint,  you  will  have  another  dish,  i.  e.,  “filet  de  bceuf,”  one 
of  the  best  dishes  ever  served.  The  ribs  is  preferred  to  the 
sirlojn  by  epicures;  called  in  French  “cotelette  de  bceuf,”  but 
it  is  too  large  to  serve  whole.  If  a  sufficiently  large  joint  is 
cut,  the  chump  end  to  roast.  For  the  servant  or  “helper,” 
or  as  a  dinner  for  a  large  family  when  economy  is  essential, 
the  buttock  of  beef  is  excellent,  and  very  profitable.  It  is 
cheaper  than  the  other  roasting  portions  of  the  ox;  has  no 
bones,  and  affords  quantities  of  rich  gravy.  But  it  should  be 
hurtg  for  some  time  until  quite  tender.  The  round,  aitch¬ 
bone  and  silver-side  are  usually  salted  and  boiled.  The  neck 
is  used  for  making  soup  or  gravy  “jus;”  ask  for  it  as  “gravy 
beef;”  the  thin  flank  is  the  part  to  be  collared.  , 

A  rump  steak”  is  to  be  ordered  for  frying,  etc.  A 
beefsteak  does  for  stewing,  etc.  The  inferior  and  cheaper 
parts  of  beef  make  excellent  “bouilli” — a  dish  for  which 
you  will  find  directions  below.  Beef — a  great  many  savory 
preparations  are  made  from  this  marvelous  animal.  Ribs  of 
beef,  tenderloin,  rib  or  sirloin  steak,  chateau  briand,  filet  mi- 
gnon.  There  are  four  different  special  preparations  such  as 
braised,  boiled,  broiled,  roasted  or  saute,  which  are  served 
with  a  different  variety  of  sauces  and  garnishings,  which  is 

—  476  — 


the  art  of  French  cooking.  Beef  exceeds  the  total  of  all  other 
meat  used  in  the  preparation  of  food  in  civilised  countries. 

Veal.  Veal  should  be  small  and  at  least  6  or  8  weeks  old, 
and  the  kidney  well  covered  with  fat,  or  the  flesh  will  be 
coarse.  If  yeal  iis  large  it  should  be  cheaper,  as  it  is  less 
delicate.  The  ,  flesh  should  be  dry,  closely  grained,  and  not 
white;  if  it  is  moist  and  clammy  it  is  stale, .  and  not  fit  for 
,  cooking.  T  ■  •?.<-,  T  •; 

The  tenderloin,  rack  or  loin  and  leg  are  best  for  ,  broiling, 
roasting  or  saute.  The  shoulder  and  breast  is  sometimes  used 
for  braising  or  roasting  in  very  small  families,  but  it  is 
usually  stewed,  as  is  also  the  knuckle;  or  the  knuckle  may  be 
broiled  or  braised;  sweet-breads  are  considered  a  delicate 
.  aliment  for  children  and  convalescent,  and  consequently  are 
•expensive.,  ;  j ,  ,  •  y  j  ..  / 

Calves’  feet  and  head  are  also  valuable,  boiled,  stewed,  or 
used  for  jelly,  and  contain  a  large  substance  of  .gelatine  and 
are  very  good  given  to  invalids.  Calves’  liver  is  prepared 
in  a  great  many  styles,  but  the  simplest  way  is  the  .  best: 
fried  or  broiled.  Veal  bone  makes  the  best  stock  for  rich 
soups  and  gravies.  It  is  a  most  useful  meat  for  made  dishes 
of  all  kinds,  on  account  of  its  delicate  flavor.  - 

Mutton.  Whether  mutton  is  of  the  best,  may  be  known 
by  its  having  a  knob  of  fat  on  the  upper  part  of  the  leg.  It 
should  be  dark  colored  and  have  plenty  of  fat.  The  color 
is  important,  as  it  is  a  proof  of  age,  and  the  older  mutton  is 
the  better  it  is.  It  should  properly  be  the  flesh  of  a  sheep 
four  or  five  years  old,  to  be  in  perfection,  but  such  meat  is 
rarely  to  be  bought  at  a  butcher’s;  one  tastes  it  only  at  the 
houses  of  country  gentlemen  who  kill  their  own  animals. 

All  the  joints  of  lamb  or  mutton  may  be  roasted.  The 
saddle  is  the  best,  the  haunch,  called  in  French  “Baron” 
saddle  and  leg  roast  undivided,  the  leg  and  loin,  separated, 
.are  the  best  "joints;  chops  are  cut  from  the  .rack  or  loin; 
.  cutlet  from  the  thick  end  of  the  loin,  best- end  of  the  neck, 
or  middle  of  the  leg.  French  chops  the  best  part  in  the 
rack  called  “cotelettes  dans  la  noix,”  nicely  shaped  and  trim¬ 
med.  The  breast  shoulder  is  sometimes  roasted  or  braised  or 
broiled,  but  is  very  good  for  stew  with  the  neck. 

The  “house  lamb”  (which  is  in  season  before  Christmas 
and  Easter)  is  most 'naturally  prepared  roasted  or  broiled. 

—  477  — 


G  ANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 

Pork.  Buy  pork  of  a  respectable  butcher  as  there  are  so 
many  different  styles  of  preparing  pork.  Get  it  from  some 
place  where  you  know  it  has  been  carefully  fed;  from  a 
dairy  farm  is  the  best  place,  or  from  a  country  farm,  as 
diseased  or  bad  pork  is  a  very  dangerous  food. 

Then  prepare  it  yourself,  and  get  your  choice,  such  as 
sausage,  blood  pudding,  ham  and  pig’s  feet,  etc.  The  fat  of 
pork  should  be  firm,  and  the  lean  white,  and  finely  grained. 
The  rind  or  skin  thin  and  smooth.  If  the  flesh  is  clammy  to 
the  touch  the  pork  is.  bad.  If  the  fat  has  kernels  in  it  the 
pig  has  been  measly,  and  the  meat  should  not  be  eaten. 
Pork  should  be  perfectly  sweet  to  be  good,  therefore,  do  not 
hang  it  long. 

Hams  are  tried  by  sticking  a  knife  or  cleaver  into  them 
up  to  the  knuckle;  if  when  drawn  out  it  has  a  nice  smell,  the 
ham  is  good.  A  bad  scent  will  be  perceived  if  it  is  tainted. 

Bacon.  If  bacon  is  good  the  rind  is  thin,  the  fat  firm  and 
pinkish,  the  lean  tender  and  adhering  to  the  bone.  Rusty 
bacon  has  yellow  streaks  in  it. 

The  roasting  joints  of  pork  are  the  leg,  shoulder,  loin 
and  rack.  The  other  joints  are  salted,  the  leg  may  also  be 
cured  and  boiled.  The  sides  or  flitches  are  made  into  bacon 
or  salt  pork.  The  leg  makes  a  ham.  The  head  to  the  tail  is 
very  good. 

Suckling  pig  is  a  young  one  of  six  or  eight  weeks,  which 
has  been  nourished  with  milk.  The  suckling  pig  is  served 
whole,  stuffed  and  roasted;  also  boned  and  carved  raw, 
cooked  with  aromates  and  served  in  jelly. 

Venison.  A  deer  is  cut  in  four  portions:  Haunch,  back 
and  breast,  shoulder,  neck. 

..Venison  is  not  very  often  bought,  but  when  it  is  you 
can  tell  to  its  being  “high”  or  not  by  running  a  cleaver  into 
■the  shoulder  and  observing  the  scent  on  it  when  withdrawn. 
The  fat  should  be  thick  and  clean.  If  the  cleft  of  the  haunch 
is  smooth  and  close  the  animal  is  young. 

Moose,  bear,  reindeer,  wild. boar  are  the  same.  Leg  and 
saddle  for  roasting  or  broiling,  shoulder  and  neck  for  stew. 

TO  CHOOSE  POULTRY  AND  GAME 

>  Poultry.  Domestic  tufkeys,  chicken,  duck,  geese,  pigeons, 
nares,  partridges,  .pheasants,  etc.,  are  .divided  into  two  classes 

—  ■478  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


—young  and  old.  The  young  ones  are  distinguished  by 
their  soft  feet,  soft  cartilage  at  the  tip  of  the  breast  bone,  the 
skin  is  moist,  smooth  and  fine  textured. 

Turkey.  The  cock  bird,  when  young,  has  a.  smooth 
black  leg  with  a  short  spur.  The  eyes  are  bright  and  full, 
and  the  feet  supple,  when  fresh;  the  absence  of  these  signs 
denotes  age  and  staleness;  they  may  be  judged  by  the  same 
rules.  Young,  roasting  or  broiling;  old,  boiling  or  braizing. 

Chickens.  The  young  cock  has  a  smooth  leg  and  a  short 
spur;  when  fresh,  the  meat  is  close  and  dark.  Hens,  when 
young,  have  smooth  legs  and  combs;  when  old,  these  will  be 
rough.  A  good  capon  or  fattened  female  chicken,  7  to  8 
months  (Poularde)  has  a  thick  belly  and  large  rump,  a  poll 
comb,  and  a  swelling  breast. 

Spring  chicken  is  best  to  roast,  broil  or  saute.  Capon, 
roast  or  braize;  “Poularde,”  roast  or  braize.  Hens  for  boiling. 

Ducks.'  May  be  selected  by  the  same  rules.  The  wing 
should  be  tender,  the  fat  more  yellow,  and  the  webbing  of 
medium  texture,  not  as  in  the  case  of  old  ducks.  Spring 
duckling,  roast  or  broiled;  old,  for  braizing  or  stew. 

Geese.  In  young  geese  always  select  plump  firm  geese. 
The  feet  and  bills  will  be  yellow  and  free  from  hair.  When 
fresh,  the  feet  are  piable;  they  are  stiff  when  stale.  Young, 
roast  and  broil;  old,  braize  or  stew. 

Pigeons  and  Squabs.  Young  pigeons  or  squabs  have  pink 
legs,  and  light  red  flesh  on  the  breast;  the  old  ones  are  dark. 
Squabs  are  young,  tame  pigeons,  and  are  generally  raised 
for  market  purposes.  They  are  as  large  as  old  ones,  but 
are  covered  with  pin  feathers,  and  in  quality  resemble 
young  ones  when  fresh,  have  supple  feet,  and  they  will  be 
firm;  if  discolored  they  are  stale.  Squab,  roasting  or  broiled; 
old,  braized  or  stewed. 

DIVERS  GAME 

Game.  Pheasants  may  be  chosen.  The  young  birds  are 
known  by  the  short  or  round  spur,  which  in  the  old  is  long 
and  pointed.  Young,  broiling  or  roasting;  old,  braizing. 

Partridges.  Yellow  beaks  and  dark  legs  are  signs  by 
which  a  young  bird  may  be  known;  a  rigid  vent  when  fresh. 

—  479  — 


CANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


When  this  part  is  green  the  bird  is  stale.  Young,  roasting 
or;  broiling;  old,  braizing.  '  ;  "  Y  .  >, 

Moor  Game.  Grouse,  wood  cocks,  snipes,  quail,  ortolans, 
plover,  red  birds,  rail  birds,  etc.,  may  be  chosen  by  the  rules 
given  above.  Roasting  and  broiling,  sometimes  saute. 

Venison  is  dark  red,  with  some  white  fat.  Roasting, 
broiling  and  sauteing.  ’  *  ■  '■  •  •' 

Hares,  rabbits,  opossum,  raccoon,  squirrel,  etc.  When  a 
hare  is  yoUng  and  fresh,  the  cleft  in  the  lip  is  narrow,  the 
body  stiff,  and  the  claws  are  smooth  arxi  sharp;  old  and 
stale  hares  will  be  the  opposite  of  this,  In  order  to  ascertain 
whether  a  hare,  etc.,  is  young  or  old,  turn  the  claws  side" 
ways;  if  they  crack  it ,  is  young.  The  ears  also  should  be 
tender  and  capable  of  bending  easily.  Roasting,  sauteing  or 
stewing.  ,  >,  ,•< 

Fish.  The  eyes  of  fresh  fish  are  bright,* .  the  gills  of  a 
fine,  clear  red,  the  flesh  very  firm,  the  body  stiff,  the  scales 
bright  and  gleaming,  and  the  entire  fish  life-like,  and  the 
smell  not  unpleasant.  Fresh  fish  is  practically  odorless,  ah 
though  sea  fish  always,  more  or  less,  have  an  odor  of  the 
sea.  Unscrupulous  dealers  dip  tainted  fish  or  meats  in  salicy- 
late  or  chloride  of  soda,  which  will  restore  fish  that  is  not 
extremely  fresh,  but  it  is  never  so  good  as  when  it  has  not 
been  kept.  Always  be  careful  in  your  buying.  The  salmon 
or  similar  fish,  the  flesh  firm  and  the  scales  very  bright.  Do 
not  buy  soles,  halibut,  herring,  mackerel,  etc.,  unless  quite 
fresh,  and  do  not  attempt  to  keep  them  even  till  the  next 
day.  Salmon,  cod,  bass  may  be  kept  twenty-four  hours. 

Eels  should  be  bought  alive;  trout  the  same.  Crabs  and 
lobsters  should  be  heavy  and  very  stiff.  Never  cook  a  dead 
crab  or  lobster,  nor  eat  any  canned  ones,  as  they  are  gen¬ 
erally  indigestible  and  sometimes  poisonous. 

Oysters.  The  oyster  is  an  ancient  aliment  of  first  choice. 
They  are  good  only  in  the  months  with  “r,”  that  is  to  say, 
from  September  to  April.  The  fecundity  of  the  oyster  is 
remarkable.  The  oyster  is  hermophrodite  and  hatches  eggs 
two  of  three  times  in  the  year,  producing  about  two  million, 
young.  The  oyster  should  be  eaten  alive,  and  it  is  strange 
to  say  that  it  is  one  of  the  rare  animated  things  that  man 
will  eat.  .  ;  -  7  i.  ■. 

The  American  people  are  masters  in  the  art  to  eating 
oysters,  especially  cooked  ones.  Every  city  has  its  specialty. 

—  480  — 


C  U  L  IN  ARY  EVOL  U  T  I  O  N:> 


Let  us  eat  these  oysters  “in  every  style.”  Oysters,  if 
fresh’,  wilT  close  forcibly  on  the  knife  when  opened:  If  the 
shell  gapes  in  the  least  degree,  the  oyster  is  losing  freshness. 
When  the  fish  is  dead  the  shell  remains  open.  Small  oysters 
are  the  best  for  eating;  for  sauces  or  other  culinary  purposes 
the  larger  kinds  are  good  enough.  *  - 

Eggs.  Shake  the  eggs;  if  they  are  bad  they  will  rattle. 
But  I  think  the  best  plan  is  to  put  them  in  a  basin  of  water, 
and  see  if  they  lie  on  their  sides,  down  in  it.  If  the  egg 
turns  upon  its  end,  it  is  bad;  if  it  lies  obliquely,  it  is  only 
“not  quite  fresh”  but  may  do  for  pudding,  etc.  The  safest 
way  is  to  held  them  up  to  the  light,  forming  a  focus  with 
your  hand.  Should  the  shell  be  covered  with  small  dark 
spots  they  are  very  doubtful.  If,  however,  in  looking  at 
them  you  see  no  transparency  in  the  shells,  you  may'  be  sure 
they  are  rotten  and  only  fit  to  be  thrown  away.  The  most 
precise  way  is  to  look  at  them  by  the  light  of  a  candle;  if 
there  are  no  spots  upon  the  shells,  and  they  have  a  brilliant 
light  yellow  tint,  they  are  fresh. 

Eggs  are  an  excellent  aliment  for  children,  also  for  sick 
and  feeble  people.  They,  however,  must  be  absolutely  fresh. 
They  lose  part  of  their  nourishment  if  they  are  hard  boiled. 

Eggs  swallowed  raw  or  eaten  soft  boiled  are  the  best. 
Also  scrambled,  fried,  poached  and  in  very  soft  omelet. 
Boiled  two  or  three  minutes  (Mollet  boiled  five  minutes  and 
peeled).  Eggs  most  used  for  culinary  purposes  are  those  of 
the  domestic  chicken, 1  duck,  turkey,  guinea  hen,  pheasant, 
ostrich  and  divers  birds;  plovers,  etc. 

A  chicken  egg  weighs  two  ounces.  The  greatest  delicacy 
in  fish  eggs  are  those  of  the  sturgeon,  which  are  used  for 
fresh  and  salted  caviar.  The  eggs  of  shad  and  herring  are 
eaten  cooked.  The  eggs  of  reptiles  are  rarely  eaten  raw 
and  used  in  alimentation,  only  turtle  and  terrapin  are  eaten
