cooked,  soft  boiled  or  in  omelet,  highly  spiced. 

Vegetables.  Take  care  that  they  are  fresh  looking  and 
crisp.  Fresh  vegetables  constitute  a  part  in  culinary  art,  as 
there  is  always  a  new  method  of  preparing  them.  The  pre' 
paration  of  the  same  vegetable  changes  each  month,  accord' 
ing  to  its  age  and  how  tender  it  is.  One  should  know  im' 
mediately  .upon  looking  at  green  peas  the  time  required  in 
cooking.  Each  vegetable  has  its  own  particular  properties, 
either  fresh  or  dry.  All  green  vegetables  should  be  cooked 

—  481  — 


CANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


in  boiling  water  and  salt,  and  kept  boiling  continually.  If 
green  vegetables  are  not  eaten  immediately,  put  in  a  basin 
of  cold  water  and  keep  refreshing  them  to  prevent  turning 
yellow  or  black. 

Potatoes.  There  are  at  least  120  different  varieties.  Every 
country  has  its  special  variety.  Very  large  potatoes  are 
empty  and  watery.  There  are  two  principal  methods  of 
preparing  potatoes  and  all  of  the  others  are  their  derivatives. 
Baked  or  fried,  bcjiled  or  steamed.  The  less  water  a  potato 
has  the  better.  The  farinaceous  potato  is  the  most  nourishing, 
and  should  be  well  cooked.  As  an  aliment  it  is  of  medium 
value,  but  it  contributes  largely  to  function  the  respiratory 
organs. 

Fruits.  In  choosing  fruits,  be  guided  by  the  weight.  The 
heaviest  are  the  best,  and  those  should  always  be  selected  in 
any  kind  of  fruit.  First  class  fruit  makes  a  delicious  break', 
fast;  fruits  prevent  or  remove  constipation,  and  often  act  like 
a  charm  upon  body  and  mind.  Eaten  with  good  bread, 
nothing  is  equally  palatable  or  wholesome.  Also  berries, 
with  bread  and  milk,  and  good  apples,  baked  or  raw.  Apples 
keep  the  entire  year,,  on  dry  straw  in  a  dry  place,  and  pears, 
hung  up  by  the  stalk,  of  the  best  varieties,  can  be  kept  till 
the  appearance  of  strawberries  the  next  year.  Pears  and 
plums  can  be  kept  the  year  round  as  easily  as  apples,  and 
summer  fruits  by  bottling  can  be  kept  perfectly  fresh  a  year, 
and  by  the  use  of  hot  houses,  fruit  can  be  picked  from  the 
trees  in  winter  or  spring.  We  can  also  preserve  them  and 
make  into  jellies.  Yet  this  process,  besides  deteriorating 
their  flavor,  impairs  their  digestibility.  Preserves  are  too 
rich.  Their  nutrition  is  too  much  concentrated.  Most  kinds 
of  fruits  can  be  dried  and,  thus  kept,  though  this  process 
dries  out  much  of  its  goodness,  as  well  as  sweetness.  Yet 
dried  fruit  stewed  is  far  better  than  none.  Yet  after  all, 
nothing  equals  simple  bread  and  choice  fruits,  if  people 
only  knew  it,  both  for  health  and  luxury. 

Green  fruit  is  most  pernicious.  Nor  do  we  realize  how 
many  children,  especially,  lose  their  lives  directly  or  indirectly 
thereby.  When  it  does  not  kill  immediately  it  often  deranges 
the  stomach,  breeds  worms,  and  induces  other  diseases. 

Foreign  fruits  are  good,  but  indigenous  are  better.  Nature 
adapts  the;  product  of  every  climate  to  its  dietetic  require' 
ments. 

—  482  — 


CULINARY  EVO  LUT I O  N 


HINTS  TO  THE  MISTRESS  OF  THE  HOUSE 

Housekeeper  or  Cook.  This  word  has  been  sadly  misused 
as  an  excuse  for  idleness,  dissipation,  and  ignorance  in  domes' 
tic*  matters.  We  will  hope  that  the  advancing  progress  of  the 
age  will  teach  its  bearers  to  unite  the  useful  virtues  of  “lady” 
of  older  days  with  the  charms  and  graces  of  a  more  inteb 
lectual  and  cultivated  period. 

On  the  “lady”  of  the  house  devolves  the  task  of  provid' 
ing  food  for  her  household;  it  should  be  her  care  that  no 
waste  or  ignorant  misuse  should  squander  the  property  of  her 
husband — most  frequently  the  bread'winner  for  the  family — 
and  that  nothing  is  lost  because  of  carelessness  or  bad  cook' 
ery.  She  is  to  take  care  that  there  is  no  lack  through  fault 
of  hers,  nor  any  drawback  to  domestic  comfort  through  im 
judicious  rule;  no  neglect  caused  by  the  love  of  idle  pleasure. 
“Lady”  means  a  “breaker  or  divider  of  bread,”  and  is  very 
significant  of  the  true  duties  of  a  woman.  It  is  the  watchful 
eye  of  the  mistress  that  keeps  the  home  beautiful  with  the 
freshness  of  cleanliness  and  the  calm  of  repose,  and  through 
her  vigilance  alone  will  the  servants  prove  faithful  in  the 
performance  of  their  duty.  It  is  her  thoughtfulness  that 
provides — very  often  her  self'denial  that  purchases — the  com' 
fort  of  others. 

The  mistress  of  a  family  commands  daily  a  small  realm 
of  which  she  is  queen.  Let  her  rule  with  justice,  meekness, 
and  quietness.  The  most  self' governed  person  will  always 
govern  best,  and  we  should  have  fewer  bad  servants  if  they 
were  all  under  the  firm  and  patient  training  of  an  employer 
who  understood  what  their  duties  really  were,  and  required 
the  best  fulfillment  of  them,  compatible  with  the  frailty  of 
human  nature.  Good  temper,  patience,  and  a  knowledge  of 
domestic  matters,  come  first  therefore  in  the  list  of  require' 
ments  for  a  model  housewife. 

After  thestf,  we  shall  name  “early  rising,”  which  is  very 
important.  A  lady  will  find  it  best  to  give  her  servants 
orders  for  the  day,  “before  breakfast,”  if  convenient,  and, 
to  do  this  well,  she  must  visit  the  larder  herself  early  that 
she  may  judge  of  what  is  required.  Her  cook  should  have 
a  book — slate  and  pencil,  and  enter  ,  the  orders  for  dinner  on 
one  leaf  of  it  that  there  may  be  no  mistakes  made  in  them. 
On  the  other  leaf  she  should  be  ordered  to  enter  daily  the 
amount  of  milk,  bread,  etc.,  bought  on1  the  previous  day, 

—  483  — 


CANCEL’S  C UL INAR  Y  E  N  CYCLOPEDIA 


which  the  mistress  should  then  transfer  to  her  commonplace' 
book,  to  be  entered  in  the  account'-book  at  the  end  of  the 
week.  Pay  all  your  bills,  “if  possible,”  every  week.  If  some 
few  remain  to  be  paid  quarterly,  do  not  on  that  account  delay 
to  make  up  the  week’s  accounts.  You  will  then  be  sure  of  your 
expenditures.  Take  care  that  the  butcher  always  brings  a  ticket 
of  weight  with  the  meat,  fish,  butter,  eggs,  and  have  those 
weight  tickets  brought  to  you  weekly  in  order  that  you  may 
compare  them  with  the  entries  in  your  book.  All  meat  etc., 
brought  into  the  house  should  be  weighed  to  see  if  the 
ticket  is  correct,  and  for  this  purpose  a  pair  of  scales  should 
be  kept  in  the  kitchen;  groceries,  etc.  should  also  be  tested 
as  to  weight,  on  receipt  of  them. 

Ascertain  the  price  of  every  article  of  food  in  your  neigh' 
borhood,  as  prices  differ  with  localities,  and  that  which  might 
be  economical  food  in  one  market  is  'frequently  the  reverse 
in  another.  In  order  to  leirn  prices,  do  not  disdain  to 
market  for  yourself,  if,  you  are  your  own  cook  or  house' 
.keeper.,’  'You  will  thus  be.  able  by  personal  observation  to 
learn  which  are  s  the  best  markets  (  fbr  different  articles,  and 
what  are  the  fair  rates  of  payment  for  them.  It  is  also, 
essential  that  a  cook  or  housekeeper  should  know  the  average 
weekly  consumption  of  food  for  each  person  in  an  ordinary 
family,  that  she  may  be  able  to  check  waste  and  provide 
pr.ov'isidps  for,  any  ,  period  she  mayj  desire.  ,  .  ,  , 

DUTIES  OF  HEAD  WAITERS,  WAITERS,  BUTLERS 
!/  '  AND  SERVANTS 

The  Breakfast.  A  taste  and  nicety  are  very  perceptible 
at  the  breakfast  table.'  One  should  never  allow  a  soiled 
tablecloth  to  appear  on  it.  The  linen  should  be  fresh  and 
snowy  white,  the  silver  brightly  cleaned,  the  tea,  coffee  or 
chocolate  pot  nicely  made,  and  if  possible,  fresh  flowers  and 
fruit  in  season  should  adorn  the  table.  A  nfcelydaid,  pretty 
appetising  breakfast  , is  a  great  promoter  of  good  temper  and 
digestion  of  those  who  have  to  submit  to  such  domestic 
inflictions.  Let  out  homes  ever  be  bright,  sunny  and  charm' 
ing;  and  that  spell  may  be  the  case  open  the  day  with  a 
‘  cheery  and  welbarranged  breakfast  table. 

At  the  head  of  the  table  place  the  breakfast  cups  and 
saucers;  the  tea  cups  at  the  left  hand  side;  the  coffee  cups  at 
the  right  hand.  The  teapot  or  coffeepot  will  stand  in  front 

—  484  — 


of  the  urn,  the  cream  or  hot  milk  to  the  right.  First  serve 
the  cereals  or  frujts.  Afterwards  put  hot  plates  by  the. 
broiled  ham  or  bacon,  chops,  eggs,  meat,  and  smaller  plate, 
knife  and  fork  to  each  person.  Bread  should  be  put  On  a 
silver  platter.  Salt  cellers  occupy  the  four  corners.  Hot  rolls 
should  be  brought  in  covered  with  a  table  napkin.  Dry 
toast  should  never  stand  longer  than  five  minues  before  it  is 
served.  Buttered  toast  becomes  soggy  and  greasy  if  too  long 
kept  before  it  is  served.  ,  ,  '  , 

•  The  Luncheon.  The  waiter,  butler  or  man  servant  has 
care  of  the  dining  room.  He  lays  the  cloth,  puts  a  water 
bottle  or  caraffe,  with  ,  glass  on  it,  at  each  corner  of  the  table, 
a  salt  cellar  and  two  tablespoons  at  each  corner;  a  small  knife; 
and  two  small  silver  forks  and  one  or  two  dessert  spoons  for 
each  person  round  the  table;  a  carving  knife  and  fork, at  the 
top  and  bottom  of  the  table,  and  a  tablespoon  near  every 
side  dish.  The  cruet-stand  on  the  table,  near  it  two  decanters 
of  wine,  a  tumbler1'  and  two  wine  glasses  to  each  person. 
Luncheon  differs  from  dinner.  It  consists  of  one  warm  dish, 
cold  meat,  with  poultry.  In  the  general  absence  of  soup  or 
fish  only  the  cold  tray,  pie,  biscuits,  cake,  eclairs,  are  all  put 
on  the  table  at  the  same  time,  but  if  a  hot  pudding  is  served 
the  waiter  brings,  it  in  afterward  when  required.  The  waiter 
should  be  ready  to  ,  push  in  the  clair  on  which  a  lady  seats 
herself  as  she  sits  down.  He  carries  round  the  luncheon 
plates  once,  and  then  goes  to  early  diqner  (unless  there 
should  be  a  luncheon  party).  He  takes  the  luncheons  away. 

The  head  waiter  or  the  butler  is  at  the  head  of  the  men 
servants.  He  has  care  of  the  dining  room,  the  plate  and  the 
drink.  He  looks  to  the  laying  of  luncheon,  and  waits  until 
the  guests  or  the  family  are  helped.  He  offers  wine,  etc., 
and  then  leaves  the  dining  room.  Home  luncheons  must  be 
directed  by  the  mistress  of  the  family,  according  to  her 
means. 

The  dinner.  Three  different  styles:  “a  la .  Franqaise,”  “a 
la  Russe,”  “a  l'Anglaise.”  If  the  dinner  is  French  style,  all 
dishes  are  served  ready  in  the  kitchen,  each  dish  according 
to  the  guest.  At  Russian  style  dinner,  the  butler  carves  at  the 
side  table  and  serves  on  the  dinner  plate.  At  English  dinner 
style,  he  sets  the  first  dish  on  the  table,  then  he  takes  his 
place  behind  his  master  on  the  left  hand  near  the  side  board, 
after  removing  the  cover  and  handing  it  to  the  waiter.  He 

—  485  — 


CANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


waits  at  the  table  with  general  supervision  over  the  other 
assistants. 

When  the  soup  and  fish  are  done  with  he  rings  the  bell 
for  the  removers,  gives  the  dishes  to  the  waiter,  receives  the 
removers  and  places  them  on  the  table.  He  removes  the 
covers  and  again  stands  near  the  sideboard,  unless  he  is 
required  to  assist  in  handling  the  entrees,  which  he  must  do 
if  there  is  only  a  second  man  to  wait.  He  draws  forward  the 
dessert  on  the  table  and  hands  it  around.  He  lays  the  dinner 
on  the  sideboard  before  dinner.  He  must  arrange  the  plates, 
glasses,  etc.,^  so  that  he  may  have  everything  ready  to  his 
hand.  He  must  lay  the  cloth  very  evenly.  The  waiter  will 
look  to  the  plate  and  wine.  On  each  dessert  plate  he  must 
put  a  doilie,  finger  glass  and  silver  knife  and  fork.  They 
will  then  be  ready  for  use  when  required.  If  ice  cream  is  to 
be  served  the  ice  plate  with  a  doilie  beneath  it,  and  also  an 
ice  spoon.  He  must  put  at  each  corner  of  the  table  two 
tablespoons,  a  caraffe  of  water  and  a  salt  cellar;  a  plate  for 
each  person,  with  a  folder  table  napkin  with  a  roll  in  it; 
two  large  knives,  one  silver  and  one  steel;  butter  knife;  the 
silver  fish  knife  and  fork,  and  a  tablespoon  for  soup;  two 
dessert  spoons,  by  each  plate.  Then  he  places  the  wine 
glasses,  etc.  The  tumblers  are  kept  on  the  sideboard  till 
required  at  dinner  parties.  If  it  is  a  large  party  there  will 
be  a  caraffe  and  glass  and  a  tiny  salt  shaker  between  every 
two  people,  also  at  dinners  a  “menu”  on  a  stand  between 
every  two  guests. 

At  ordinary  dinners  cold  relishes  are  put  on  the  table 
after  the  soup  or  fish.  The  waiter  receives  the  dishes  from 
the  kitchen.  When  they  are  put  on  the  table,  the  waiter 
places  the  first  dish.  The  waiter  stands  behind  the  lady  of 
the  house.  He  carries  round  plates  of  soup,  offering  one 
to  each  person.  He  must  hand  it  on  the  left  hand  of  the 
diner. 

Next  he  hands  the  fish,  then  the  entrees  or  side-dishes, 
then  the  removes  or  chief  joints,  etc.  Wine  is  offered  directly 
after  soup,  and  frequently  during  dinner.  Sherry  for  soup, 
white  wine  for  the  fish  and  claret  for  meat  are  used  at  the 
ordinary  table.  Hock  or  champagne  is  carried  around  iim 
mediately  after  the  first  entree  at  a  dinner  party.  The  waiter 
must  stand  watching  the  table  very  attentively,  so  that  he 
may  change  every  plate  as  soon  as  required.  He  must  be 

—  486  — 


CULINARY  EVOLUTION 


careful  to  learn  from  the  kitchen  which  are  the  right  sauces 
to  hand  with  the  different  dishes. 

The  second  course  is  brought  in.  The  roast  poultry  or 
game  is  handed  first;  afterward  the  sweets,  changing  the 
plates  as  required.  The  cheese,  butter,  etc.,  are  next  served 
and  handed  round. 

The  Dessert,  Etc.  We  need  only  refer  our  readers  for 
modes  of  serving  desserts  to  our  colored  plate  or  cut  glass 
in  which  the  fruit  is  shown  in  one  of  the  beautiful  services 
peculiar  to  the  present  day.  But  we  must  add  that  between 
and  amongst  the  dishes  it  is  usual  to  put  small  glass  dishes 
or  stands  for  diners.  Preserves,  olives,  almonds,  candies, 
or  any  sweet  dishes  are  given  in  our  dessert  recipes.  A  few 
wine  biscuits  should  be  put  on  the  table  and  olives  or  salted 
almonds  are  sometimes  required  for  the  gentlemen. 

The  Supper.  A  lady  has  full  space  for  exercising  her 
taste  at  the  supper  table.  Hot  or  cold  fancy  dishes  ready  in 
the  buffet  according  to  the  season.  A  good  eye  for  color 
will  give  a  great  charm  to  the  arrangement.  With  flowers, 
fruit,  silver  urn  for  consomme,  coffee  and  hot  water  for  tea, 
chafing  dishes  for  hot  meats.  Divers  cold  meats  in  jelly  or 
aspie,  salad  bowls  or  dishes,  and  all  the  elegance  of  sweet 
dishes.  She  can  scarcely  fail  to  offer  a  perfect  picture  of 
gastronomic  beauty  to  the  eye,  if  «h<»  will  take  a  little  care 
in  the  arrangement  of  the  table  herself.  A  plan  drawn  out 
on  paper  and  given  to  an  intelligent  butler  or  waiter  will 
secure  her  from  mistakes  generally.  If  her  butler  or  servant 
is  inexperienced  she  should  first  glance  at  the  table  herself 
before  her  guests  are  invited  to  it.  For  two  or  three  evening 
guests,  a  few  sandwiches,  a  cake,  fruit  and  wine  will  suffice, 
as  people  now  generally  dine  late,  but  not  sufficient  for 
ordinary  entertainments.  Naturally  according  to  your  resources. 


—  487  — 


GAN'CEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


THE  VALUE  AND  LAWS 
OF  HEALTH 

(La  Sante) 

(By  the  celebrated  French  Dr.  Raspail.) 

Happiness  is  the  constitutional  and  only  legitimate 
product  of  every  organ  of  the  body,  every  faculty  of  the 
mind,  every  element  of  Our  being.  Pleasure  in  quaffing, 
luxuriantly  the  fresh  air  of  heaven  and  then  in  expend¬ 
ing  the  vitality,  thus  obtained,  is  the  only  natural  func¬ 
tion  of  respiration.  Benevolence  was  created  to  bless 
the  needy,  pour  the  oil  of  consolation  into  the  wounded 
soul,  avoid  causing  pain,  and  adorn  human  nature,  as 
well  as  to  render  the  giver  himself  also  happy,  it  being 
still  more  blessed  to  give  than  to  receive. 

Value  of  health 

Therefore  exceeds  that  of  anything  else  because  it 
imports  the  greatest  attainable  zest  and  relish  to  life 
and  all  its  blessing.  Nor  can  we  enjoy  life,  except  by 
its  instrumentality  and  in  proportion  to  its  vigor.  With¬ 
out  it,  whiat  can  we  do  or  become,  or  enjoy?  When  de¬ 
cease  has  destroyed  appetite,  the  most  delicious  fruits 
and  food  only  nauseate;  yet  how  much  a  keen  appetite, 
consequent  on  excellent  health,  relishes  them?  Well 
might  the  glutted  epicure  offer  the  beggar  boy  a  guinea 
for  his  morning’s  appetite.  The  rich  invalid  is  poor 
because  he  can  not  enjoy  his  possession,  yet  the  healthy 
are  therefore  rich,  because  their  fund  of  life  and  cap¬ 
abilities  of  being  happy  are  great.  Those  who  have 
always  enjoyed  health,  little  realize  its  uses  or  value. 
As  we  measure  time  by  its  loss,  so  we  rarely  estimate 
the  blessihgs  of  health  till  it  declines.  Oh,  I  would  give 
my  all — all  the  world  if  mine — for  the  repossession  of 
that  health — life — I  have  carelessly  and  wantonly 
squandered,  and  that  without  having  received  any  value 


_  488 


THE  VALUE  AND  DAWS  OF  HEALTH 


in  return  for  this  choicest  gift  of  heaven.  Then  what 
consumate  foolishness  to  trifle  with  health  as  almost 
all  do  now?  Esau’s  folly  was  wisdom  in  comparison 
with  those  who  carelessly  give  away  a  lifetime  of  vigor 
for  one  of  feebleness— who  even  barter  life  itself  for 
some  momentary  indulgence.  Let  then  the  preservation 
of  health  be  the  great  concern,  the  paramount  business 
of  life,  as  it  is  the  perfection  of  wisdom  and  the  great 
instrumentality  of  enjoyment  Since  health  consists  in 
the  normal  and  vigorous  action  of  all  the  physical 
functions,  its  preservation  of  course  consists  in  their 
preservation  and  its  restoration  in  their  restoration. 

“Meat'  gives  force  and  strength.” 

Brute  force  it  does,  but  of  this  men  has  relatively 
too  much  already,  as  we  shall  soon  show.  “Would  you 
then,”  it  is  farther  objected,  “have  us  abstain  from 
flesh,  and  thus  become  as  pusillanimous  as  the  Hin¬ 
doos?” 

But  are  the  meat  eating  Indians  and  Laplanders  so 
very  forcible?  What  have  they  ever  accomplished — 
what  triumph  ever  achieved  other  than  with  the  scalp¬ 
ing  knife  and  the  tomahawk?  If  meat  alone  gives  force 
one  Indian  would  master  two  pale  faces  whereas  one 
white  man  is  equal  to  a  score  of  red  ones.  The  former 
eat  less  meat,  yet,  under  every  disadvantage  has  driven 
the  latter  back  and  back  again,  farther  and  still  farther 
upon  the  setting  sun,  till  they  bid  fair-foul?  to  exterm¬ 
inate  his  race.  Admitted  that  meat  gives  force,  yet 
mark  the  kind  of  force  it  imparts.  Analogous  to  that 
of  the  tiger  and  wolf — force  to  dare  and  kill  rather 
than  to  do. 

Does  the  lion  accomplish  so  much  more  than  the 
horse?  Or  is  the  wild  bull  so  extra  tame  and  feeble? 
Do  not  both  the  strongest  and  the  fleetest  of  animals 
live  upon  vegetables?  The  elephant  and  rhinoceros 
eat  no  meat,  yet  their  muscular  power  and  endurance 
far  transcend  those  of  the  lion  and  the  tiger.  The 
deer,  antelope  and  gazelle  live  on  herbage,  yet  distance 
all  flesh-eating  animals  in  the  open  chase.  Since,  there¬ 
fore,  the  fleetest  and  the  strongest  of  animals,  eat  no 


489  — 


GAN  CEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


meat,  must  man  eat  It,  or  be  weak  or  sluggish?  Or  tc 
apply  this  principle  directly  to  man :  Is  the  Highland 
Scotchman,  who  was  brought  up  on  oatmeal,  and  tasted 
meat  no  oftener  than  the  moon  quartered,  so  very  in¬ 
efficient?  Are  the  potato-fed  Irish  weak?  Can  our 
own  beef-gourmands  dig  or  carry  more?  Try,  you  meat 
advocates.  The  rice-fed  Chinese  will  outdo  “John  Bull” 
and  “Uncle  Sam”  except  in  shedding  blood.  So  will 
the  herbenious  inhabitants  of  the  Pacific  Isles.  Not 
that  animal  food  does  not  develop  muscular  strength. 
Carnivorous  animals  are  strong,  but  herbiverous  ani¬ 
mals  are  stronger,  yet  have  less  propensity.  Hence 
since  meat  develops  propensity,  yet  it  is  not  necessary 
to  either  strength  or  force— since  it  animalizes  and  de¬ 
praves,  and  thus  does  a  positive  damage,  but  not  a 
necessary  good— why  injure  ourselves  by  consumption? 

HYGIENE,  DIET,  LONGEVITY 

“Cuisine,  c’est  la  mddecine;  c’est  la  mede- 
cine  preventive  la  meilleure” — J.  Michelet. 

Dieting  consists  in  abstaining  from  certain  foods,  and 
it  is  very  necessary  to  follow  a  hygienic  diet  in  certain 
diseases.  Every  mistress  of  the  house,  and  also  the 
cook  should  know  how,  and  what  to  do  in  an  emergency 
-  case.  -■ 

Meat  shortens  people’s  lives  by  decades.  I  mean 
that  people  who  wish  to  live  a  century,  must  not  eat 
meat — especially  the  highly  proteid  meats.  The  whole 
secret  of  perpetual  youth,  is  a  complete  supply  of  food, 
to  all  the  cells  of  the  body  combined,  with  ridding  the 
cells  of  all  waste  matter.  To  accomplish  this,  you  must 
cut  out  food  excess  proteids,  and  cut  down  cereals, 
including  bread,  and  eat  less  sodium  or  common  salt. 

Second:  eat  plenteously  of  food  containing  potassium 
salts.  You  will  find  them  in  all  kinds  of  salads.  Water 
cress  is  one  of  the  best  elixirs  of  life.  Consume  more 
•  organid  acids,  which  -are  best  obtained  from  grapes, 
raisins,  oranges,  apples  and  citrons.  Acids  remove 

—  490  — 


HYGIENE 


waste  from  the  arteries,  soften  them,  and  reopen  the 
blood  vessels.  Any  man  showing  signs  of  old  age, 
should  take  three  months  of  this  diet,  and  he  would 
become  young  again.  Lastly  eat  garlic  and  onions, 
the  greatest  intestinal  cell  antiseptic  in  the  world,  with¬ 
out  being  destructive  like  drugs.  (Read  each  article 
on  vegetables.)  They  will  cure  the  most  malignanw 
skin  and  blood'  diseases  known.  Two  ounces  of  garlic 
daily  is  the  greatest  preventative  of  old  age. 

Another  great  fault  of  mankind  is,  that  all  drink  too 
much.  I  do  not  mean  alcoholic  beverages,  but  too  mucn 
coffee,  tea,  milk,  besides  beer,  wine  and  spirits.  It  is 
not'  what  is  drunk,  but  how  much.  Too  much  fluid 
lessens  a  man's  power  of  resistance.  For  instance  things 
growing  wet,  develop  rapidly  and  then  die.  Hook  at 
the  water  lily.  Things  growing  in  dry  places  take  longer 
to  mature,  are  much  healthier,  and  live  longer.  Look 
at  the  oak.  The  same  principles  apply  to  human  beings. 
I  am  not  for  prohibition — I  believe  in  wine  and  beer. 
Every  man  and  woman  of  more  than  fifty  should  have 
a  glass  of  wine  daily;  it  dilates  the  capillaries  of  the 
body,  preventing  the  closing  of  the  blood  vessels.  A 
glass  of  beer  daily  helps  a  person  after  forty.  Hard 
drink  is  wholly  bad.  I  am  against  alcohol  in  any  form, 
for  any  one  under  forty;  it  is  unnecessary.  Getting 
intoxicated  once  a  month  does  not  hurt  according  to 
Raspail,  the  great’  French  doctor,  eating  the  greatest 
steak  once  a  week  does  not  hurt— but  the  steady  drinker 
and  meat  eater  wears  down  the  body's  vitality,  fills  his 
cells  with  waste  matter,  and  hardens  the  arteries. 
Georges  Clemenceau,  the  “Old  Tiger  of  France”,  owes 
his  wonderful  mentality  and  activity  to  his  diet  of 
vegetables  and  milk,  and  in  going  to  bed  early,  and 
arising  early. 

So  it  is  very  necessary  to  follow  a  practical  diet,  to 
aid  or  cure  certain  diseases,  in  following  the  se  different 
culinary  preparations. 

ALIMENTATION  FOR  CHILD 

The  new  born  baby  only  nurses  and  sleeps,  and  only 
attention  is  paid  to  that  during  the  first  few  months. 


GANCEL’S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


The  habits  of  the  child  .should  be  regulated  early.  As 
the  child  grows  older,  it  should  not  sleep,  so  much  in. 
the  day  time,  but  the  sleep  at  night  should  be  the  same. 
As  the  mother  is  the  principal  doctor  for  her  child, 
she  should  pay  attention  to  its  food.  Naturally  children 
prefer  cakes  and  Candies  to  soup  and  bread.  They 
should  be  fed  regularlyn  and  at  a  certain  hour.  Never 
give  a  child  any  spiced  food,  nor  too  much  sugar;  the 
food  should  contain  more  salt  than  sugar.  Children 
shold  be  taught  to  use  a  spoon  in .  the  right  hand,  and 
the  fork  in  left,  the  bread  to  be  taken  in  the  right  hand. 
A  knife  should  never  be  given  to  a  child  before  the  age 
of  6  or  7  years.  The  mother  should  pay  great  attention 
to  the  physical  education  of  the  child,  and  table  hygiene. 
A  well  bred  child  or  children  will  always  give  a  good 
deal  of  pleasure  to  the  parents.  The  celebrated  French 
writer  J.  J.  Rousseau  says  of  children:  If  a  mothes 
wishes  to  sbeil  her  children,  let  her  give  them  every¬ 
thing  they  ask  for,  and  then  it  is  the  mother  who  will 
suffer  afterwards. 

/POISONING  (Empoisonnement) 

Ptomaine  poisoning  is  caused  frequently  by  mussels, 
oysters*  crabs,  fish,  pork,  and  ice  cream;  and  very  often 
mushrooms. 

First:  Take  awTay  promptly  the  poison. 

Second:  To  take  the  poison  out  of  the  system  give 
the  patient  a  vomitif,  130  grains  of  ipecacuanha  in  a 
glass  of  water.  For  piirgatif  1  tablespoonful  of  magne¬ 
sia  in  a  glass  of  water  with  a  little  sugar.  Give  an 
enema,  1%:  quarts  of  warm  water  and  2  tablspoonsful 
of  kitchen  salt.  If  the  person  does  not  die  immediately 
from  poisoning,5  then  life  can  be  saved  with  this  remedy. 
If  you  have  not  any  of  these  medecines  in  the  house 
give  the  patient  milk  with  the  white  of  eggs  to  drink 
and  an  anemea  with  warm  water  and  salt. 

INDIGESTION  (Indigestion) 

Indigestion  is  Caused  by  food  not  being  well  prepared, 
but  sometimes  by  having  taken  cold,  or  by  some  great 
moral  suffering,  in  cases  of  indigestion,  take  camomille 


—  492  — 


HYGIENE 


tea,  flavored  with  orange  water.  If  this  method  does 
not  stop  the  indigestion  take  a  vomitif. 

INTOXICATION  (Ivresse) 

It  is  said  that  to  prevent  intoxication,  take  a  quarter 
of  a  glass  of  pure  olive  oil  or  sweet  almond  oil,  be¬ 
fore  eating. ,  The  best  method  of  all  is  to  drink  moder¬ 
ately,,  but.  sometimes  it  depends  upon  the  disposition  of 
the  stomach,  and  one  glass  of  champagne ..  might 
intoxicate. .  Black  coffee  and  a  few  drops  of 
ammonia  in  Seltzer  aids  intoxication,  as  it  arouses 
the  nervous  system.  Give  the  following:  water 
4  ounces,  essence  of  peppermint  y2  ounce,  liquid  am¬ 
monia  12  drops.  Mix,  and  give  every  quarter  of  an 
hour,  two  or  three  times ;  or  sugar  and  water  with  a  few 
drops  of  ammonia,  or  Bromo  Seltzer. 

THE  DIGESTION  (La  Digestion) 

.Fortunately  for  the  civilization  of  today,  we  have  fire 
to  cook  our  food.  The  advance  made  in  culinary  art 
has  transformed  everything. 

It  is  to  this  science  that  civilization  owes  its  re¬ 
gulated  meals,  which  is  the  regime  of  good  digestion. 
Medical  science  has  classified  certain  aliments,  by  its 
degree  of  digestibility,  comparing  the  human  body  to 
a  wheel,  which  the  fonctionment  accepts  and  refuses* 
certain  matters.  Think  well  ,and  you  will  digest  well. 

Necessiary  time  for  completing  digestion  of  certain 
aliments.  (Temps  necessaire  pour  la  digestion  complete 
des  principaux  aliments) :

BILL  OF  FARE  (Menus) 


A  FEW  MODELS  OF  BILL  OF  FARE 


It  is  impossible  to  arrange  exactly  any  bill  of  far 
here,  because  every  hotel  or  restaurant  has  its  ow 
style,  according  to  the  business  and  importance  of  tb 
house..  To  facilitate  the  work  and  render  quick  service 
the  name  of  the  dish  should  be  written  very  distinctl 
on  the  menu  or  card.  My  opinion,  and  also  the  opinio: 
of  a  good  many  of  my  colleagues,  is  that  the  kitche 
of  today  merits  more  respect  and  should  be  class©- 

arts,  and  every  care  should  be  taken  ‘ti 
facilitate  tne  daily  task  of  the  cook.

Mrs.  Wilson  has  asked  me  to  acknowledge  the  re¬ 
ceipt  of  the  book  you  have  sent  to  her  and  wishes  me 
to  express  her  warm  thanks  for  your  courteous  thought

EDITH  BENHAM 

Mr.  Joseph  Gancel  Secretary 

745  Pine  Street 

San  Francisco,  California 


LOUIS  SHERRY 
New  York 

June  12th  1918. 

Mr.  J.  E.  Gancel 
San  Francisco,  Cal. 

My  dear  Mr.  Cancel: 

i  wish  to  thank  you  for  your  thoughtfulness  in  send¬ 
ing  me  the  “Petite  Encyclopedia  Culinaire”,  which  I 
received  with  your  letter  of  June  5th.  I  know  that  it 
will  prove  very  useful  and  interesting. 

Again  thanking  you,  with  best  wishes  I  am 

Sincerely, 

LOUIS  SHERRY

THE  WHITE  HOUSE 
Washington,  D.  C. 

My  Dear  Sir: 

Mrs.  Wilson  has  received  your  letter  and  the  menu 
book,  for  both  of  which  she  wishes  me  to  express  her 
many  thanks.  Unfortunately,  all  of  the  reports  in 
regard  to  her  giving  receipts  for  publication  have  been 
erroneous,  but  it  gives  her  much  pleasure  to  accept 
your  little  book.  Believe  me, 

Very  truly  yours, 

ISABELLA  L.  HAYNES, 
Mr.  J.  Gancel,  Secretary. 

Georgian  Terrace  Hotel, 

Atlanta,  Ga.

CANCEL'S  CULINARY  ENCYCLOPEDIA 


THE  NEW  WILLARD 
Washington,  D.  C. 

Mr.  Joseph  Gancel,  October,  21st,  1910. 

Dear  Sir: 

I  have  found  your  book,  “Menu  Terms,”  ■which  you 
kindly  sent  me  sometime  ago,  of  great  assistance  ana 
would  like  to  obtain  two  more  copies.  Please  send 
by  mail  or  express  and  upon  receipt  of  bill  will  remit 
for  same. 

If  you  have  new  editions,  please  let  me  know.  Upon 
my  next  visit  to  New  York.  I  will  try  and  drop  in  and 
see  you.  Yours,  truly, 

C.  E.  S'CHAFFNER, 
*  Steward. 


THE  PLAZA 
New  York  City 

M.  Joseph  Gancel,  Le  11  Decembre  1910. 

New  York. 

Cher  Monsieur, 

J’ai  bie  requ  votre  brochure  “Menu  Terms”  dont  je  vous 
remercie  infiniment.  Je  trouve  cet  ouvrage  interessant,  tres 
utile;  il  fait  honneur  a  son  auteur,  que  je  felicite  sincerement. 
Veuillet;  agreer,  Monsieur  Gancel,  mes  civilites  empressees. 

E.  LAPERRUQUE. 


HOTEL  KNICKERBOCKER 
New  York  City 

M.  Joseph  Gancel, 

New  York  City. 

Cher  Monsieur, 

J’ai  bien  requ  le  “Ready  Reference  of  Menu  Terms”  que 
vous  m’aves  envoye.  Accepter,  cher  monsieur,  mes  meilleurs

pour  votre  Encyclopedic  que  je  trouve  tres  precieuse. 

A  vous  bien  sincerement.  j  MALNATI
